In unshard_parameters() and reduce_gradients() (per-microbatch hot path), symm_mem.rendezvous(buffer, group) is invoked on every call whenever _symm_mem_pool is not None. rendezvous is a collective/handshake operation intended to be done once per persistent buffer at setup time; calling it on every all-gather/reduce-scatter re-establishes symmetric-memory handles each step. If PyTorch does not internally short-circuit an already-rendezvoused buffer, this adds a redundant collective + host overhead on every forward/backward step, negating the intended symmetric-memory speedup. Because _unsharded_model_weight storage is resized to 0 and reallocated each step (release_storage/reallocate_storage), the buffer pointer/registration may also churn, forcing genuine re-rendezvous. This should be hoisted to buffer construction time (once) rather than per-step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-step FSDP train loop with use_symm_mem=True vs False; measure per-step time and count symm_mem rendezvous/handshake calls (torch profiler CPU + CUDA) across N>1 steps to confirm rendezvous is not repeated each step.

findings.claim: The old pipeline created a bounded output queue when streaming (asyncio_Queue(maxsize=self.buffer_size if request.streaming else 0)) and used buffer_size to create backpressure for balanced generation in a multi-task setting. The new _RolloutPipeline replaces this with an explicitly UNBOUNDED output_queue (comment: 'Unbounded: flow control is owned entirely by the submission gate'). While the submission gate does cap inflight work, in the prevent_dataset_reorder=='B' consumption path stage_consume only releases the gate one full batch at a time (release_after('consumed') after popping a completed batch). If groups within a batch complete out of order, completed groups pile up in output_queue and in self._consume_pending dict without any second-level backpressure, and buffer_size (the old streaming-balance knob) is removed entirely. In a multi-task streaming setting this removes the cross-task balancing backpressure the old buffer_size provided, allowing memory growth / unbalanced generation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming grouped rollout with multiple tasks and skewed per-group latency; measure peak output_queue/_consume_pending size and cross-task fairness vs old buffer_size backpressure

findings.claim: This revert re-introduces an unconditional torch.cuda.empty_cache() call inside save_checkpoint_and_time(). empty_cache() forces a full CUDA device synchronization and returns all cached (unused) allocator blocks to the driver. Every subsequent training iteration after a checkpoint save must then re-request those blocks via cudaMalloc, which is a slow, serializing driver call. This matches the historical 'allocator-config' pattern where empty_cache() is used to reduce fragmentation/memory pressure at the cost of a per-invocation sync + subsequent re-allocation stall. Because it runs on the checkpoint save path (not every iter), the cost is amortized, but for frequent checkpointing it adds a hard sync + allocator churn each save. The design intent (documented in the added comment) is to free GPU headroom for the async checkpoint worker's D2H transfers -- a deliberate memory-vs-speed tradeoff being reinstated.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-checkpoint wall time and the iteration time of the first N steps immediately following a checkpoint save, with and without the empty_cache()/free_overlap_buffers() block, at a realistic checkpoint interval.

findings.claim: The added loop calls model_chunk.free_overlap_buffers() which frees the overlap param-gather buffers before the async checkpoint save. free_overlap_buffers() wait()s on any pending param_gather_handle (a synchronization point) and Nones out the layerwise gather buffers. On the next forward pass these overlap buffers must be re-allocated, and the pending-gather wait() can serialize against otherwise-overlapped param all-gather comm. This is the checkpoint-time analogue of the deferred-free / re-allocation cost: memory is released to lower the async-save peak, but at the price of re-allocation and a comm-drain sync afterward. Only impactful when overlap_param_gather is enabled (distributed/layer-wise optimizer).
findings.suggested_benchmark: With overlap_param_gather enabled, compare param-gather overlap efficiency (GPU idle bubble) on iterations directly after a checkpoint save vs steady state.

In GroupedRolloutGenerator.get_grouped_rollouts, the new batch-consumption path replaces the previous 'decrement + submission_gate.release()' retry (which re-suspended on await submission_gate.acquire()) with an unbounded `while not await generate_and_enqueue(batch_id, 0): pass` loop. When filter_groups_with_same_reward rejects a group (np.std(rewards) <= 1e-6), this loop retries immediately with no backpressure gate acquisition in between, so a task holding the submission slot keeps re-issuing group_rollout->agenerate calls back-to-back. Under a stream of low-variance (identical-reward) groups this can spin generating rollouts far faster than they are consumed, wasting inference compute and holding the semaphore slot. The old code released the gate on rejection and looped back through acquire(), naturally yielding/backpressuring.
findings.suggested_benchmark: GRPO streaming rollout with grpo_filter_groups_with_same_reward=True and a reward function producing many zero-variance groups; measure inference calls/sec and event-loop responsiveness vs parent.

findings.claim: Draining the predecessor's reduce-scatter via finish_grad_sync() at the successor's dispatch time inserts a blocking grad_reduce_handle.wait() on the compute/current stream before the successor's own collective is issued. This is the intended memory tradeoff (freeing the pinned all-to-all output buffer earlier to lower peak memory), but it can serialize predecessor communication with successor dispatch and reduce comm/compute overlap during the backward pass — potentially introducing bubbles if the predecessor RS has not yet completed when the successor becomes ready. Scoped to reduce_scatter_with_fp32_accumulation only, limiting blast radius.

The `scales` allocation is O(total_tokens) GPU work per forward call (and per layer) with an unconditional CUDA kernel launch/allocation each step. Since total_tokens (batch*seq) is constant across steps for a given microbatch shape, this is cacheable one-time setup work leaking into the hot path — matching the one-time-setup-on-hot-path prior. Consider caching/reusing these buffers keyed on total_tokens/device/dtype like `_recipe` and `_fused_impl` already are.

findings.claim: The default CUDA graph token-count distribution is switched from dense linear ([1,2,4] + range(8,256,8) + range(256,max+1,16), i.e. potentially hundreds of captured sizes with 8/16-token granularity) to EXPONENTIAL (halving: max, max/2, max/4, ... => only ~log2(max) sizes) and this becomes the default for auto-sizing (num_cuda_graphs == -1). At runtime a real batch is padded up to the nearest captured token_count that is >= it (is_applicable_for_batch_dim uses token_count >= real token_count). With exponential spacing, a batch of e.g. 130 tokens is padded up to 256 (worst-case ~2x padding) instead of to 136 under the old linear grid. This is exactly the historical 'warmup-shape-gap' mechanism: captured shapes are coarser, so many decode/mixed steps run a much larger graph than needed, wasting compute per step. The change is default-on (EXPONENTIAL is the default enum), so users get the coarser grid without opting in.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode/mixed throughput and per-step latency with num_cuda_graphs=-1 on a workload with token counts landing just above a halving boundary (e.g. 129, 257, 513); compare EXPONENTIAL vs LINEAR padding overhead.

findings.claim: cuda_graph_sizing_distribution defaults to EXPONENTIAL in InferenceConfig, and dynamic_context.py forwards inference_config.cuda_graph_sizing_distribution unconditionally. This is a static default that selects the coarser (log-spaced) capture path for all existing users who don't set the new arg. Per the historical config-toggle-perf-feature pattern, a static default value routes to a path with different (here up to ~2x) padding overhead; workloads previously benefiting from the dense linear grid regress in per-step compute unless they explicitly pass --inference-dynamic-batching-cuda-graph-sizing-distribution linear.
findings.suggested_benchmark: A/B decode throughput with the new default (exponential) vs prior behavior (linear) at fixed max_tokens, measuring average padded token_count per step.

findings.claim: The new dual-stream shared-expert overlap in NVLSAllGatherVDispatcher.dispatch_preprocess launches `apply_module(self.shared_experts)(hidden_states)` on SharedExpertMLP.stream via `with torch.cuda.stream(stream)`, but `hidden_states` is allocated on the main (current) stream and is NOT protected by a `record_stream(stream)` call. This is exactly the mechanism seen in the historical case (#26440/344a0017c: `with torch.cuda.stream(...): shared_output = self.shared_experts(hidden_states.clone())` with no record_stream). Although a `stream.wait_stream(current_stream())` is issued before launch (so the side-stream reads see valid data), there is no ordering that keeps the main stream from FREEING/REUSING the hidden_states memory once its own consumers (the `.view(...)` reshape and AGV dispatch on the main stream) retire. The caching allocator tracks allocations per creating stream; once the main stream's last use completes, the buffer can be handed back and overwritten by another main-stream allocation while the shared-expert GEMM on SharedExpertMLP.stream is still reading it. The correct fix is `hidden_states.record_stream(stream)` after entering the side stream. This is a correctness-adjacent memory race that also manifests as intermittent gpu-util-or-bubble / non-determinism.
findings.suggested_benchmark: NVLS inference MoE decode/prefill with --moe-shared-expert-overlap enabled; run under compute-sanitizer memcheck and stress-test with allocator churn (many concurrent layers) to surface the missing record_stream race.

The new shared-embedding bucket emission in _compute_per_buffer_param_layout gives each shared (tied) embedding its own isolated bucket where only shard 0 holds the embedding and shards 1..dp_size-1 are filled with same-size *empty padding* slots. This multiplies the buffer footprint of every shared embedding by dp_size: padding cost per shared embedding is (dp_size-1)*pad_to_divisor(numel, shard_divisor). The diff's own comment quantifies this as ~7*(vocab*hidden) elements for a 128k x 8192 embedding at dp_size=8 — many GB in the param buffer and again in the grad buffer. This is a genuine layout-time over-allocation introduced by this PR (previous ping-pong sharding kept whole params without inflating buffer size). While the invariant (no param crosses a shard boundary) forces some padding, isolating shared embeddings with no size-matching is the worst case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure param+grad buffer memory footprint (allocated bytes) for a model with a tied vocab embedding across dp_size in {2,4,8}, comparing legacy ping-pong sharding vs new layout-based sharding.

The new `disable_ep_consensus` branch turns the engine's idle path into a busy-spin. In the parent code the RUNNING loop always went through `_ep_establish_consensus` (which yields cooperatively) and, in genuinely idle states, the loop yielded with `await asyncio.sleep(0.02)` (dynamic_engine.py:2384/2387/2401), throttling CPU when there is no work. The new `disable_ep_consensus` path replaces this: when `local_pending == 0` it runs `controller.dummy_forward()` + `step_end_event.synchronize()` every iteration and only does `await asyncio.sleep(0)` (a bare yield with no delay). This is exactly the historical busy-poll shape ('while True: dummy step; await asyncio.sleep(0)') — an idle EP>1 (or even EP==1 with the flag) engine now spins the event loop and issues a full dummy GPU forward every ~few hundred microseconds even with zero requests, burning CPU and GPU cycles when idle. The unit test added (test_disable_ep_consensus) even asserts `idle_calls > 0` in a 0.2s window, confirming the intended busy-spin behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Idle-engine CPU/GPU utilization: start DynamicInferenceEngine with disable_ep_consensus=True and no requests; measure host CPU% and dummy_forward() call rate over 5s vs the consensus path (which sleeps 20ms between idle iterations).

This is the same mechanism as the retrieved cases: swapping an autotuned/table-driven config for a fixed heuristic. The chosen config is not device-, N-, K-, or dtype-specific. Because M = num_tokens_hint (a per-step *estimate*, not the actual token count) is used to bucket M, and the same config is reused for both FC1 (N=fc1_out) and FC2 (K reduction over fc1_out), the geometry may be suboptimal for real shapes — potential per-GEMM latency regression on inference decode/prefill relative to the autotuned baseline, especially on GPUs/shapes never validated against these fixed picks.

findings.claim: SymmetricMemoryManager._default_size_mb was doubled from 256 to 512 MB. This default is used by every buffer created without an explicit size_mb (e.g. the shared 'tp' buffer at inference_layers.py:176/295/402/506/536, which never passes size_mb). Each keyed buffer is a resident symm_mem.empty(numel, uint8, 'cuda') allocation, so any 'tp' NVLS user now permanently reserves 512 MiB of symmetric GPU memory instead of 256 MiB, per process group. This is a resident-memory increase on the inference path, not a computed size. Note the MoE dispatcher buffers now pass explicit size_mb (via _size_mb) so they are unaffected, but the default bump silently raises the floor for the TP path.

The new FA4 varlen paths hardcode `num_splits=1`, whereas the pre-existing FA2/FA3 kvcache path used `num_splits=0` (auto-tuning heuristic that lets the kernel choose the optimal split-K to saturate the GPU for memory-bound decode). `num_splits=1` disables split-K parallelism, which can under-utilize SMs and increase decode latency for small batch / long-context inference. Unlike the batch_invariant_mode case (where num_splits=1 is intentional for determinism), here it is applied unconditionally in the FA4 path regardless of batch_invariant_mode. If FA4 supports num_splits=0 auto-tuning, this is an unnecessary throughput/latency regression on the FA4 fast path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Dynamic-batching decode latency/throughput on Blackwell with FA4 installed, small batch + long KV, comparing num_splits=1 vs num_splits=0.

This diff is the CORRECT resolution of the historical kernel-fusion/suboptimal-kernel-gate pattern rather than a regression. Previously forward_cuda fell back to forward_native (`torch.square(F.relu(x))`), executing as two unfused elementwise kernels (relu, then square). The new forward_cuda dispatches to a single fused custom CUDA op (torch.ops._C.relu_squared), and forward_native is still preserved for the Inductor path (custom_ops='none' -> dispatch_forward returns compiled forward_native, which Inductor fuses). The benchmark explicitly compares custom kernel vs native_compiled. No gating regression: enabled() -> forward_cuda when custom ops on; forward_native (compiled) when Inductor on. The `if current_platform.is_cuda_alike(): self.op = ...` guard is consistent with sibling ops (NewGELU/FastGELU/QuickGELU) and does not break the CPU/XPU native fallback.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmark_relu_squared.py (custom vs native_compiled vs native)

findings.claim: The new default behavior of `_flashinfer_autotune_skip_ops` returns `{"fp4_gemm"}` whenever a `FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel` is present, causing `flashinfer_autotune` to pass `skip_ops={"fp4_gemm"}` and thus SKIP autotuning of the CuTeDSL NVFP4 GEMM by default. Per this same file's own docstring, "Without autotuning, FlashInfer will rely on heuristics, which may be significantly slower." This is the config-toggle-perf-feature pattern: the default config value now routes the fp4 GEMM to a heuristic fallback tactic instead of the autotuned-best tactic, potentially reducing NVFP4 linear throughput at inference time. The tradeoff is intentional (avoids per-tactic JIT compile cost during warmup), but the runtime kernel choice may be sub-optimal versus tuned. Whether it is a net regression depends on how close the CuTeDSL heuristic fallback is to the best tuned tactic for the served shapes.

findings.claim: The TileLang monitor wraps `JITImpl.__call__` (the per-invocation runtime dispatch path), unlike the Triton/CuTeDSL monitors which hook only the compile entry points (`jit_post_compile_hook` / `cute.compile`) and thus fire only on actual compilation. As a result, `_call_with_monitor` runs `_tilelang_cache_miss_key(self, args, kwargs)` on EVERY kernel call — including cache hits (the common steady-state case). That helper does: `dict(kwargs)` copy + `pop`/`update` (`_tilelang_call_kwargs`), a full `func.parse_args(*args, **call_kwargs)` re-parse, and, when `jit_impl.mode == 'auto'`, an `_infer_jit_mode(...)` + `func.set_mode(...)` on every call, followed by a cache lookup — all wrapped in try/except. This adds constant per-call Python overhead to the inference hot path for every TileLang kernel dispatch while the monitor is active, whereas the intent is to detect only the rare post-warmup (re)compile event. Consider hooking a compilation-only entry point (analogous to the Triton/CuTeDSL design) so that cache-hit dispatches pay no diagnostic cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark: with jit_monitor active, repeatedly invoke a cached TileLang JITImpl kernel (steady-state cache hits) and compare per-call latency vs. monitor inactive; also test mode=='auto' path where _infer_jit_mode runs each call.

Grouped-MoE GEMM: block_m is now hard-coded to 64 for ALL regimes (was regime-dependent: block_m=128 for prefill T>=1024, 64 for decode via _mxfp8_moe_tiles). _select_cfg for the grouped GEMM only tunes BLOCK_N/BLOCK_K/GROUP_SIZE_M/num_warps/num_stages and never varies BLOCK_M off 64. For large-prefill token counts, a BLOCK_M=64 tile produces more (finer) M-tiles and doubles the number of A/scale re-reads per expert relative to the former BLOCK_M=128 prefill tile. moe_align_block_size also pads each expert to block_m=64, and with GROUP_SIZE_M=4 swizzling the redundant-A-reread argument only partially offsets the loss of the larger M-tile. Unlike the dense linear _select_cfg (which is fully M-bucketed with BLOCK_M up to 128/256), the grouped path lost its prefill BLOCK_M scaling — a potential prefill-throughput regression on large MoE token counts. Recommend confirming the measured A/B covered a true prefill T (>=1024) shape, not just decode.

findings.claim: The new persistent unified score buffer `self.unified_scores_buffer` is allocated as `[max_num_batched_tokens, num_index_heads, MAX_K_TILES=8192]` fp32 in `MiniMaxM3IndexerMSAMetadataBuilder.__init__`. This is a very large fixed allocation whose tile dim is hardcoded to 8192 (1M-token context) regardless of the actual `max_model_len`/`max_seq_len`. E.g. with max_num_batched_tokens=8192 and 32 index heads, that is 8192*32*8192*4B = ~8.6 GiB of persistent VRAM per builder; even at 4 heads it is ~1 GiB. In the parent, the MSA prefill scored into an fmha-native `max_score` tensor sized to the plan's *natural* `max_k_tiles` (cdiv of the actual max_seq_len), so the buffer scaled with real context length. Forcing `plan['max_k_tiles']=MAX_K_TILES` and pre-allocating 8192 tiles wastes memory whenever real context << 1M tokens.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak VRAM of the M3 MSA indexer builder with a modest max_model_len (e.g. 32k) and typical head count vs the parent; confirm the unified buffer is ~8192/cdiv(max_seq_len,128)x larger than needed.

findings.claim: `build()` now calls `unified_scores.fill_(_SCORE_SENTINEL)` on `self.unified_scores_buffer[:num_tokens]` every forward. This zeros/writes a `[num_tokens, num_index_heads, MAX_K_TILES=8192]` fp32 region each step even though the score kernels only ever write the causally-valid tiles per row (cdiv(seq_pos+1,128) tiles), which for short/decode sequences is a tiny fraction of 8192. The fill touches num_tokens*num_index_heads*8192*4 bytes of DRAM per forward regardless of actual context length — a per-step memset of up to several GiB. This is the classic 'wasted work on an over-sized buffer' pattern: the sentinel is only needed for the *unwritten* tail up to each row's num_valid_pages, not out to 8192 tiles. Bounding the fill (or the buffer) to cdiv(max_seq_len,128) tiles would cut this DRAM traffic proportionally.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench a decode-heavy M3 step: time `build()` (or the fill_) with MAX_K_TILES=8192 vs a fill bounded to cdiv(max_seq_len,128); expect the memset to dominate build() at large max_num_batched_tokens.

findings.claim: GeluAndMul.forward_cpu behavior changes for NON-PowerPC CPUs (x86/ARM). Parent code: `forward_cpu` returned `self.forward_cuda(x)` (i.e. the C++ `torch.ops._C.gelu_and_mul`/`gelu_tanh_and_mul` kernel) whenever `self.op` was set, and `__init__` set `self.op` for ALL CPUs. The new `forward_cpu` returns `self.forward_native(x)` (PyTorch eager `F.gelu(...)*...`) unless the arch is POWERPC. So on x86/ARM CPUs the fast fused C++ activation kernel is no longer used and every GeGLU activation now falls back to the slower unfused PyTorch-native path (extra intermediate tensors + no fused mul). This is a per-call hot-path regression on x86/ARM CPU inference for any model using GeluAndMul. Verify whether the C++ gelu_and_mul kernel was intended to remain the x86 path.

findings.claim: In flashinfer_autotune(), the condition that disables the persistent-cache (leader-only autotune + broadcast) path was broadened from `all2all_backend in {deepep_high_throughput, deepep_low_latency, deepep_v2}` to `get_world_group().world_size > 1`. Previously, in the common distributed case (TP/PP/DP>1 without DeepEP), use_persistent_cache stayed True, so only rank 0 ran the full FlashInfer autotune benchmarking sweep (fi_utils.autotune(tune_mode=True)) and non-leader ranks did a cheap plain _dummy_run, then loaded broadcast results. After this change, ANY distributed run (world_size>1) takes the `not use_persistent_cache` branch, running the full `fi_utils.autotune()` benchmark sweep on EVERY rank. This multiplies the (expensive) autotune benchmarking work across all ranks and increases warmup/startup time, and also discards the persistent on-disk cache reuse across launches. The regression only surfaces on multi-GPU setups.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure warmup/startup time of flashinfer_autotune on a multi-GPU (world_size>1, non-DeepEP) config before vs after this change; compare rank-0-only autotune + broadcast vs full autotune on every rank.

findings.claim: In FlashInferB12xExperts.apply(), the call was changed from `flashinfer_b12x_fused_moe(..., output=output)` (which wrote the fused-MoE result directly into the pre-allocated `output` buffer) to `wrapper_output = wrapper.run(...)` followed by `output.copy_(wrapper_output)`. This introduces, on every MoE forward (hot path), (1) an extra allocation of a full [M, K] result tensor by `wrapper.run()`, and (2) an extra device-to-device full-tensor copy to move it into `output`. The prior design explicitly documented keeping apply() alloc-free (see `_fc2_input_scale` comments). The wrapper.run API no longer accepts an output-destination argument, so the copy is unavoidable given the new call shape, but it is a per-forward allocation+copy that did not exist before.

findings.claim: This diff flips the default runner selection so that ALL non-MoE dense generate models now use the V2 ModelRunner (previously only a fixed allowlist of 6 architectures did). In VllmConfig.use_v2_model_runner (vllm/config/vllm.py), the return was changed from `any(arch in DEFAULT_V2_MODEL_RUNNER_ARCHITECTURES ...)` to `any(...) or not model_config.is_moe`. This is a static-default path-selection change that routes a broad class of models onto the V2 code path by default. This matches the config-toggle-perf-feature mechanism: a default value now chooses a different execution path for a wide set of workloads. If the V2 runner has any regression (e.g., cudagraph handling), it will now be the default rather than opt-in. Notably the same PR removes the ENFORCE_EAGER=1 workaround in the model_runner_v2.yaml test (`# This requires eager until we sort out CG correctness issues`), indicating the V2 path was still shaking out cudagraph correctness — broadly defaulting to it may regress latency/throughput for models not previously covered.

findings.claim: This diff makes the ROCm aiter path (HAS_AITER_MHC and hidden_size % 256 == 0) the DEFAULT on gfx942/MI3xx, which turns OFF the fused post+pre MHC path. Concretely, DeepseekV4DecoderLayer.use_fused_mhc = HAS_TILELANG_MHC and not (HAS_AITER_MHC and hidden_size % 256 == 0). When aiter is selected, use_fused_mhc becomes False, so forward() routes to _forward_unfused_post_pre instead of _forward_fused_post_pre. The fused path uses a SINGLE fused kernel mhc_fused_post_pre_tilelang (post of prev block + pre of next block fused into one op), whereas the unfused path issues TWO separate ops per position: hc_post (mhc_post_aiter) followed by hc_pre (mhc_pre_aiter). This is the inverse of a kernel-fusion win: for hidden_size%256==0 configs on ROCm, per decoder layer the MHC post/pre now dispatch 2x custom-op launches (attn boundary + ffn boundary => 4 aiter launches) instead of 2 fused launches, adding kernel launch overhead + extra intermediate materialization (residual_cur) on the hot per-forward decode path across all layers. Whether aiter's separate kernels net faster than the fused tilelang kernel is unverified in the diff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On MI3xx (gfx942) with a DSV4 config where hidden_size%256==0, compare decode latency/throughput of the aiter unfused post/pre path (this commit default) vs the tilelang fused_post_pre path (HAS_AITER_MHC forced off), measuring per-layer MHC kernel launch count and end-to-end tokens/s.

`_all_gather_batched_symm_mem` does NOT use the persistent pre-registered scratch that the PR itself introduces for exactly this reason. It allocates fresh symm tensors inside `nccl_symm_mem_context(pynccl_comm)` on every call. Per the PR's own docstring for `_get_symm_scratch`, allocating a fresh symm tensor per collective pays the `nccl_symm_mem_context` snapshot + window-registration scan (~0.5 ms) every call. The batched list all-gather (all_gatherv over a list of tensors, used in EP/SP paths) therefore keeps the per-call registration overhead the single-tensor/RS paths eliminated — an inconsistency that leaves a hot-path collective slow.

findings.claim: The weightless RMSNorm/fused_add_rms_norm XPU path now allocates and fills a fresh `torch.ones(x.shape[-1], device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)` buffer on every call instead of dispatching to the native weightless kernel. RMSNorm runs for every transformer layer on every forward pass, so this is a hot path: each call issues an extra device allocation + fill (ones) kernel that is discarded immediately after the single rms_norm/fused_add_rms_norm invocation. This is wasted per-forward buffer work — a constant all-ones weight could be cached/created once (or the multiply skipped as the reverted native path did) rather than re-materialized every step. Note this is NOT a d2h-sync: `torch.ones(..., device=x.device)` allocates directly on the device (no CPU-list->h2d copy like `torch.tensor([...])`), so latency comes from the redundant allocate+fill kernel launches, not a host sync.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench RMSNorm forward on XPU with has_weight=False across many layers/tokens, comparing throughput before/after (native weightless path vs ones-buffer path).

findings.claim: In DeepEPHTPrepareAndFinalize._sync_dbo_comm_if_needed, on ROCm the diff inserts torch.cuda.current_stream().synchronize() immediately after both the dispatch (line ~173 in _do_dispatch) and combine (line ~388 in _finalize) calls whenever DBO is enabled. This is a full host-side blocking sync of the DeepEP communication stream on the hot per-MoE-layer forward path. The entire purpose of DBO is to overlap one ubatch's DeepEP dispatch/combine collective with the other ubatch's compute (exactly the historical missing-comm-overlap mechanism). Forcing the current stream to drain before the next DBO ubatch can proceed serializes comm against compute and defeats the cross-ubatch overlap this feature exists to provide, reintroducing the comm bubble on ROCm. Note the sync is placed on whatever the 'current stream' is at that point (the comm stream after the yield-switch), and the CPU blocking will stall the ubatch thread scheduling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DP+EP DeepEP high-throughput MoE decode/prefill throughput on ROCm with --enable-dbo, comparing tokens/s and GPU-util/bubble vs the parent (with/without the forced synchronize)

In _get_priority_backends_for_gpt_oss(), EMULATION (OCP_MXQuantizationEmulationTritonExperts) is added to the auto-selection priority list for gpt-oss MXFP4 MoE. This backend explicitly dequantizes MoE weights on the fly during EVERY forward pass (see ocp_mx_emulation_moe.py apply(): _dequantize_weights(w1), _dequantize_weights(w2) each call) and falls back to BF16 TritonExperts, and its own warning states it 'may be slower than native quantized MOE'. Because it is now a member of the auto priority list rather than being strictly opt-in via --moe-backend emulation, on hardware where the preceding native backends fail is_supported_config (e.g. MI300/MI250 non-gfx950 ROCm, or CUDA configs missing native kernels), auto selection will silently land on the slow emulation path instead of erroring/forcing an explicit opt-in. This is a per-forward dequantization hot path and a large throughput regression versus the native quantized kernels.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-oss MXFP4 MoE decode throughput (tokens/s) on MI300/MI250 (non-gfx950) with moe_backend='auto' before vs after this change; confirm whether auto now selects EMULATION vs a native backend.

The new PyNvVideoCodec GPU decode backend limits concurrency to a single decoder slot: PYNVVIDEOCODEC_MAX_RETAINED_DECODERS = 1 in vllm/multimodal/video.py. _borrow_decoder_slot() blocks on _decoder_slot_cond.wait() whenever a slot is in use, so all concurrent GPU video-decode requests in the frontend process fully serialize on this one shared decoder+stream. Because each request borrows the slot TWICE (once in _read_source_metadata for metadata and again in _decode_to_pinned_host for the actual decode), throughput under multi-request video load is capped at one decode at a time even on a large GPU. This is an opt-in backend (must set backend='pynvvideocodec'), so default opencv/pyav users are unaffected, and the byte-counting pool acquire() in decode_frames_pynvvideocodec is a second serialization gate on top of the slot. Recommend documenting/exposing the retained-decoder count so decode can scale with available VRAM rather than being pinned at 1.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrent video-preprocessing throughput with backend='pynvvideocodec' under N simultaneous requests vs opencv/pyav backends

`ClientInner::send_to_engine` (imp.rs) is changed from directly `await`ing `transport::send_message(...)` to `self.handle.spawn(async move { ... }).await?`. This is on the request-submission hot path: `EngineCoreClient::call` invokes `send_to_engine(EngineCoreRequestType::Add, &req)` for every inference request, and `call_utility`/`do_abort_requests` also route through it. Every request now incurs a cross-runtime `Handle::spawn` (task allocation + scheduler enqueue on the ZMQ runtime + a `JoinHandle.await` round-trip) instead of an inline await. Under high request concurrency this adds per-request task-scheduling latency and allocation churn to the critical send path, and hands work to the fixed 4-thread ZMQ runtime which can become a bottleneck.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark request submission throughput/latency (e.g. many concurrent /v1/completions or gRPC Generate calls) comparing inline send vs handle.spawn send; measure p50/p99 add-request latency and QPS at high concurrency.

findings.claim: The new FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel is inserted at the FRONT of the CUDA auto-selection list _POSSIBLE_NVFP4_KERNELS[PlatformEnum.CUDA], ahead of the previously-default FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel. Since its is_supported() only requires SM10x + has_flashinfer() and can_implement() unconditionally returns True, this kernel becomes the DEFAULT NVFP4 linear backend for ALL SM10x users under 'auto' — a silent hot-path backend swap for every NVFP4 quantized model on Blackwell-class GPUs. If the cute-dsl GEMM is slower than cutlass for common shapes/batch sizes, this is a broad default-path throughput/latency regression. This is exactly the historical [fused-backend-swap] pattern (defaulting NVFP4 linear to a newly-added FlashInfer backend). Recommend gating auto-selection to shapes/regimes where cute-dsl is validated faster, or placing it after cutlass in priority until benchmarked, rather than promoting it to #1 default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-layer NVFP4 linear latency/throughput of FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel vs FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel on SM100 across representative (M,N,K)/batch sizes (decode M=1..8 and prefill large M) to confirm the new default is not slower on the common path.

findings.claim: Unlike FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel, the new cute-dsl kernel does not override input_quant_key() (inherits the base returning None) and does not use as_quantized_activation(). Its apply_weights() always calls scaled_fp4_quant() inline. This means the cute-dsl path cannot participate in the activation-quant fusion that the cutlass path benefits from — every apply_weights performs a fresh input quantization step that could otherwise be folded/shared. Given this kernel is now the auto default, workloads that previously got fused/pre-quantized activations via the cutlass path lose that fusion, adding a redundant per-call quant op on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark end-to-end decode throughput on an NVFP4 model on SM100 comparing cute-dsl (no input_quant_key fusion) vs cutlass (with kNvfp4Dynamic input_quant_key) to quantify overhead of the non-fused inline scaled_fp4_quant in the new default path.

This diff is a performance FIX, not a regression. In the parent, _call_hf_processor expanded each video placeholder via `prompt = prompt.replace('<|vision_start|><|video_pad|><|vision_end|>', video_placeholder, 1)` inside the per-video loop. Each str.replace scans/rebuilds the whole prompt string (O(prompt_len)); with V videos and expanded placeholders growing the prompt, this is O(V * total_len) — quadratic in number of videos, matching the retrieved 'quadratic-python-structure' prior. The new code defers to a single token-level pass (_replace_video_token_placeholders) over the already-tokenized prompt_ids, replacing all V placeholders in one O(n) sweep. No new regression is introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Qwen3-VL multi-video prompt processing latency vs. num_videos (2/4/8/16) measuring _call_hf_processor wall time

findings.claim: This is a revert of PR #45309 ("Optimize dsv4 cudagraph by reducing eager_break_during_capture, 26.8%~27.9% E2E TTFT improvement"). The diff re-adds the @eager_break_during_capture decorator onto DeepseekV4Attention.attention_impl (line ~426). Under breakable cudagraph capture (VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH), this wrapper calls capture.add_eager(...) which ends the current captured graph segment, runs the WHOLE attention_impl (wq_b q up-proj + fused_qnorm_rope_kv_insert, indexer, MLA compressor, MLA attention) eagerly, and begins a fresh segment — for every C4A layer in the model. The parent (optimized) version kept only the inner sparse_attn_indexer custom op as the eager break and used a 2-way overlap path guarded by BreakableCUDAGraphCapture.is_active() so that wq_b+kv_insert+compressor stayed inside the captured graph. Reverting therefore restores many extra eager breaks per forward, shrinking captured graph segments and reintroducing per-layer graph-boundary launch/dispatch overhead — the exact regression #45309 removed (its measured ~27% TTFT win).
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek-V4/V3.2 (C4A layers, compress_ratio==4) TTFT with VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH=1; compare eager-break count (BreakableCUDAGraphCapture.num_eager_breaks) and end-to-end TTFT before/after this revert

The diff replaces the previous 'flush all pending jobs when all tracked requests are finished' fast-path (removed from build_connector_meta in scheduler.py) with a polling-based has_pending_push_work() that returns True while self._jobs is non-empty or manager.has_pending_work() is True. In vllm/v1/core/sched/scheduler.py:2138-2140, has_pending_push_work() is OR'd into the loop-continuation predicate (with has_unfinished_requests()), so while offload store/promotion jobs are draining the engine core keeps issuing full schedule()/execute steps even when NO requests are scheduled. This trades a single one-shot flush for repeated empty engine steps (each involving build_connector_meta, update_connector_output, worker round-trips), i.e. idle busy-stepping until offloads complete. On a quiescent engine that just finished its last request, instead of one flush the loop now spins through potentially many no-request steps until _transfer_jobs and secondary-tier work drain — added latency/CPU on the idle path. This matches the removed test test_flush_all_jobs_when_no_requests_remain and the test changes dropping expected_flushed on request finish.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine step count / wall time to fully drain offloads after the last request finishes (sync and async scheduling): compare pre/post-change number of empty schedule() iterations and time-to-idle when store/promotion jobs are still in flight.

Pure launch-tile re-tuning (same algorithm, tuned knobs): both diffs replace fixed BLOCK_M/BLOCK_N/num_warps with regime-gated tiles selected by token count (M>=1024 in linear; T>=1024 via _mxfp8_moe_tiles in MoE). This matches the historical kernel-config-tuning pattern (e.g. Mixtral/Qwen3 fused-MoE GEMM config re-tunes). The tiles/thresholds are hardcoded for gfx950 at MiniMax-M3 shapes but the kernel selector routes ANY model's MXFP8 native path on gfx950 through here — non-shape-keyed constants can regress other N/K/E shapes even though they help MiniMax-M3. Unlike the JSON per-shape LoRA/MoE config precedents, there is no shape-keyed lookup, only a single token-count threshold.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MXFP8 native MoE on gfx950 across non-MiniMax-M3 expert shapes (varying E/N/K) at both decode (T<1024) and prefill (T>=1024) token counts; compare vs parent fixed 64/128/nw8 tiles.

findings.claim: Decode-regime MoE tile shrinks BLOCK_N 128->64 and num_warps 8->4 (T<1024). For layers with large N (e.g. 2I or H), halving BLOCK_N doubles grid blocks in the N dimension and reduces per-block work/occupancy; this is a genuine change to the hot decode path. If MiniMax-M3 N happens to be small this helps, but wider N shapes on the same code path may see reduced GEMM efficiency at low batch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Grouped-GEMM MXFP8 kernel decode latency (T=1..512) for N in {1024,3584,7168} on gfx950: BLOCK_N=64/nw4 vs BLOCK_N=128/nw8.

findings.claim: This diff removes AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer and replaces it with plain make_async(self._encode/self._decode) wrappers. The removed class implemented micro-batching: it pulled pending encode/decode requests from per-op queues, waited up to batch_wait_timeout_s (2ms) to accumulate up to max_batch_size (32) requests with identical kwargs, and issued a single batched tokenizer call (self.tokenizer(prompts, **kwargs) / tokenizer.batch_decode(token_ids_list)). After this change, each request's prompt is tokenized/detokenized individually (one thread-pool call per prompt via _async_tokenizer_encode/_async_tokenizer_decode in _tokenize_prompt_async / _detokenize_prompt_async). Under concurrent front-end load (many simultaneous requests), tokenization I/O reverts to being serialized one-request-at-a-time instead of coalesced into batched tokenizer calls — the exact inverse of the historical [serial-io] microbatch optimization. Throughput regression on the OpenAI-serving hot path is expected when many short requests arrive concurrently and share the tokenizer executor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrent OpenAI /v1/completions or /v1/tokenize throughput benchmark with many simultaneous small requests (e.g. benchmark_serving with high concurrency, short prompts) comparing parent vs this commit to measure tokenizer batching loss.

`get_prefill_chunk_plan` is invoked inside `_forward_prefill` (vllm/models/deepseek_v4/nvidia/flashmla.py, replacing the old constant-chunk loop). `_forward_prefill` runs once per DeepseekV4 attention layer (~60 layers) on every forward/prefill step. The new method performs a Python-level `while` loop over `num_prefills` requests with per-element `.item()` scalar reads and per-request `max()`/comparison arithmetic to derive the adaptive chunk plan. Because the plan depends only on constant-per-step inputs (`swa_metadata.prefill_seq_lens_cpu`, `prefill_query_lens_cpu`, `prefill_window_size`, `prefill_max_model_len`, `prefill_max_num_batched_tokens`) plus `PREFILL_CHUNK_SIZE`/`compress_ratio` — which are identical across all layers of a given type within a step — this Python host loop is recomputed identically ~60x per step. Under long prefill batches (large `num_prefills`) the O(num_prefills) Python loop with per-element `.item()` scalar extraction becomes non-trivial host overhead repeated on the hot path. The chunk plan should be computed once per step (e.g. cached in the builder's metadata) and reused by all layers rather than recomputed in each layer's forward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSv4 (deepseek_v4) E2E prefill throughput with large prefill batch / many chunked-prefill requests, measuring per-step CPU time in _forward_prefill across all ~60 layers

In the per-step hot path prepare_inputs(), the diff adds `req_ids = list(scheduler_output.num_scheduled_tokens.keys())` followed by `get_num_active_loras_for_dispatch(...)`, which internally calls `lora_state.get_activate_loras(req_ids)` — a fresh Python loop over all req_ids building a set of LoRARequest — on every model step when LoRA is enabled. Note that make_lora_inputs() already computes the same active_lora_requests set (via the refactored get_activate_loras); the value here is only needed to derive its length (num_active_loras) for cudagraph dispatch. This is redundant per-iteration work: the length could be derived from the mapping already built, or the set computed once and reused. Extra list() materialization of dict keys plus the set-building loop repeats every decode step. Impact is modest (O(num_reqs)) but it is on the hottest path and only guarded by `if self.lora_config`.

In CPUAttentionMetadataBuilder.build(), the new encoder-only branch allocates and zero-initializes a fresh transient KV buffer on every step: `encoder_cache_tensor = torch.zeros((total_block_num, num_kv_heads, block_size, 2*head_dim), dtype=self.dtype)`. build() runs once per forward/decode step, so this is a per-step allocation + full zero-fill sized to the whole batch's padded block count. The parent encoder path (_run_sdpa_forward) had no KV cache buffer at all — it ran SDPA statelessly. This introduces a recurring allocate+memset on the hot metadata-build path whose cost scales with total_block_num (batch tokens/block_size). Matches the historical buffer-sizing-layout pattern (a per-step cache buffer whose scope/allocation is now materialized eagerly rather than reused from a persistent pool).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Encoder-only model (e.g. BERT-style / embedding) throughput on CPU across varying batch token counts; measure metadata build() latency and per-step allocation overhead vs the parent SDPA path.

findings.claim: The Dynamic SD feature forces cudagraph_mode to PIECEWISE at config time (_maybe_override_dynamic_sd_cudagraph_mode) whenever the schedule is used with full cudagraphs. This is a deliberate correctness-over-performance override: full cudagraph capture (which speeds up decode) is disabled, so every decode step runs piecewise, incurring a persistent throughput/latency cost. Additionally, because K now varies at runtime (0..num_spec_tokens), the effective decode query length 1+K changes step-to-step; captured/piecewise-compiled shapes assume a fixed 1+num_speculative_tokens layout, so smaller-K steps can miss the captured shape and fall back, echoing the historical warmup-shape-gap where the decode batch (BS*(1+K)) diverges from captured cudagraph sizes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode TPOT/throughput with eagle spec decode + num_speculative_tokens_per_batch_size vs static K, measuring cudagraph vs piecewise/eager fallback rate across batch sizes.

findings.claim: This diff removes the @support_torch_compile decorator from the DeepSeekV4MTP module (line ~247 in parent) while replacing hand-written RMSNorm/mask ops with hand-fused helpers (fused_mtp_input_rmsnorm, mtp_shared_head_rmsnorm) and splitting the mtp_block into mtp_block(...) + explicit mtp_block.hc_post(...). This is the inverse of the healthy kernel-fusion pattern from history (Qwen2_5_VisionBlock, where norm+residual were merged into a single fused RMSNorm). By dropping @support_torch_compile, the whole MTP forward graph is no longer captured/compiled by torch.compile, so torch.inductor can no longer fuse the surrounding elementwise/reduction ops or eliminate Python-level dispatch overhead on the hot spec-decode path. The introduced Python-side fused_* helpers only cover the enorm/hnorm/mask and shared-head norm; the h_proj/e_proj add, the hc_post recombination, and per-step dispatch now run eager. On XPU this can materially increase per-token launch/dispatch overhead relative to the compiled graph.

findings.claim: release_device_memory_under_pressure() is invoked once per quantized module inside the model.named_modules() loop in process_weights_after_loading. When the pressure condition triggers on a UMA GPU, each call performs torch.accelerator.synchronize(device) followed by torch.accelerator.empty_cache(). synchronize() fully drains the CUDA stream (blocking the host until all outstanding kernels complete), and empty_cache() releases all cached (reserved-but-unallocated) blocks back to the driver via cudaFree. Doing this per-module — potentially hundreds of times for a large model — can add substantial wall-clock overhead to weight loading, because: (1) repeated full-device synchronizations serialize the loading pipeline, and (2) freeing the caching allocator each time forces the subsequent module's process_weights_after_loading to re-acquire memory via cudaMalloc, defeating the allocator's cache reuse and inflating per-module allocation cost. This is a hot path executed once per weight-load, but the per-module frequency in the loop is the concern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model load time (process_weights_after_loading) on a UMA device (e.g. GH200/DGX Spark) for a large quantized model, comparing before/after this change, and count how many times empty_cache()/synchronize() actually fire during loading.

Because this value feeds `encoder_budget.py`/`compute_mm_encoder_budget` (via `get_mm_max_toks_per_item` -> encoder_compute_budget/encoder_cache_size) and the memory-profiling dummy run, the vision-encoder workload is now sized against a much smaller token count than the encoder actually processes. This is a config/profiling-driven sizing regression: the encoder tower is under-budgeted, potentially undersizing the encoder cache and mis-estimating peak activation memory for the vision encoder. This is a revert restoring prior behavior, so it is a re-introduction of a known under-profiling issue rather than a brand-new bug, hence low-to-moderate severity.

This commit reverts the warp-shuffle + vectorized-I/O optimization (parent commit a4b14b98c) and reintroduces the slower one-block-per-group design. Specifically, the kernel goes back to scalar per-element loads (`float gate = token_input_gate[tid]` / `token_input_up[tid]`, one element per thread) instead of the coalesced wide vector loads (`InVec`/reinterpret_cast, EPT = group_size/32 contiguous elements per lane). This is exactly the narrow-per-element-load pattern that the historical INT8-quant / RMS-norm cases flagged as memory-latency-bound.

findings.claim: `_inflight_prefill_reserved_blocks()` sums `_request_remaining_blocks(req)` over the entire `self._inflight_prefills` set each time a candidate async KV-connector load is considered in the waiting loop. Each `_request_remaining_blocks` call invokes `coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate(...)`, which itself loops over all `single_type_managers` (and per-group work). So per waiting-loop iteration with `load_kv_async=True`, the cost is O(len(inflight_prefills) * num_kv_cache_groups). If several async loads are being admitted in one schedule() step, this becomes roughly O(inflight_prefills * candidates) — super-linear in the number of concurrent in-flight prefills. This mirrors the historical 'quadratic-python-structure' pattern where a per-step recompute scales with the set size. Mitigating: it only executes on the `load_kv_async` branch (P/D disaggregation async loads), not the general decode/prefill hot path, and inflight sets are usually small. Consider caching the reservation sum and invalidating on set mutation, or computing it once per schedule() step rather than per candidate.
findings.suggested_benchmark: P/D disaggregation benchmark with many concurrent async KV-connector loads (high max_num_seqs, many WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS requests) measuring schedule() latency vs number of in-flight prefills.

findings.claim: The NIXL EPLB rewrite moves descriptor construction and transfer setup from a batched per-peer operation into per-expert work inside add_recv(). In the parent, execute() built one batched READ per source peer: it collected local_descs/remote_descs for ALL experts received from that peer and called _create_peer_xfer once (one get_xfer_descs + two prep_xfer_dlist + one make_prepped_xfer + one transfer) per peer, i.e. O(world_size) xfer-handle setups. In the new code, add_recv() is invoked once per received expert and each call does _create_peer_xfer (get_xfer_descs on local+remote descs, prep_xfer_dlist twice, make_prepped_xfer) followed by transfer(xfer_h). This is now O(num_recv_experts) NIXL descriptor allocations / handle preparations / RDMA transfer submissions per layer instead of O(num_peers), multiplying the per-layer NIXL setup cost by the fan-in of experts per peer. Since transfer_layer/move_to_buffer runs once per MoE layer on every periodic EPLB rebalance (transfer_run_periodically loop, ~65 layer iterations per rebalance), the extra descriptor churn is repeated on every rebalance cycle.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark NIXL EPLB transfer_layer end-to-end latency with high expert fan-in per peer (many recv experts sharing a source rank), comparing batched-per-peer vs per-expert xfer submission; measure get_xfer_descs/prep_xfer_dlist/make_prepped_xfer call counts and total rebalance wall time.

findings.claim: In the new Triton fast path `swap_blocks_batch` (swap_blocks_triton.py), the descriptor tensors are moved to CUDA with `.to("cuda", non_blocking=True)`. Because these buffers are pinned (allocated via `_new_descriptor_buffers` with `pin_memory=is_pin_memory_available()`), non_blocking is effective — but this is only guaranteed when pin memory is actually available. When `is_pin_memory_available()` returns False (e.g. some deployments), `_new_descriptor_buffers` produces pageable tensors and `.to(cuda, non_blocking=True)` silently becomes a synchronizing pageable copy on the transfer stream, stalling the pipeline. This is on the CPU->GPU offload path (hot for prefix/kv-offloading workloads). The C++ `cuMemcpyBatchAsync` path did not perform an extra per-transfer H2D descriptor upload; the Triton path adds three H2D copies per transfer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark swap_blocks_batch on CPU->GPU with pin_memory disabled vs enabled, comparing per-transfer latency of Triton path vs C++ DMA path.

findings.claim: The newly added PowerPC VSX FP16<->FP32 conversions (FP16Vec16(const FP32Vec16&) and FP32Vec16(const FP16Vec16&) in cpu_types_vsx.hpp) implement the conversion by spilling all 16 lanes to a stack buffer (vec_xst) and running a scalar per-element for-loop of c10::Half(float)/float(c10::Half) conversions, then reloading. These conversions sit on the hot SHM allreduce inner loop (all_reduce_sum_impl in shm.cpp constructs FP32Vec16 local_data_fp32(local_data) and vec_t reduced_data(local_data_fp32) for every vec_elem_num chunk when dtype is c10::Half). The store->scalar-loop->reload pattern defeats vectorization and adds a memory round-trip on the per-iteration critical path for FP16 all-reduce. Note this is new-platform enablement (the fast SHM path did not exist for PowerPC at parent), so it is a first-implementation efficiency concern rather than a regression against a previously-faster path; the ARM implementation (cpu_types_arm.hpp) provides intrinsic-based conversions as a reference for a faster approach (e.g. VSX xvcvsphp/xvcvhpsp on POWER9+).

findings.claim: The change gives the encoder CUDA graphs their own dedicated memory pool at runtime capture (encoder_graph_pool = current_platform.graph_pool_handle(), passed into encoder_cudagraph_manager.capture(...)), separate from the decoder/global CUDA-graph pool. This mirrors the historical 'separate CUDA graph memory pools each reserve their own memory' regression: a distinct graph_pool_handle() is a distinct CUDAGraph private mempool that does NOT overlay the main pool, so its reserved bytes are additive. The code itself acknowledges this ('Encoder graphs use a manager-local pool at runtime, separate from the decoder pool, so add their estimate instead of overlaying it' -> total_estimate = decoder_estimate + encoder_memory_estimate). Net effect: extra GPU memory permanently reserved for the encoder pool rather than sharing the existing global_graph_pool.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak/reserved GPU memory with cudagraph_mm_encoder enabled vs disabled, and compare reusing the global graph pool for encoder capture vs a dedicated pool.

findings.claim: During memory profiling, the code now performs a full real encoder CUDA graph capture (encoder_cudagraph_manager.capture(graph_pool=encoder_profiling_pool)) over every token budget just to sample memory, then discards it via clear(). Each torch.cuda.graph(graph, pool=...) context runs gc.collect()/empty_cache() internally per budget (the historical wasted-compilation-work mechanism of per-graph capture overhead). Because the persistent manager re-captures all budgets again later at real capture time, the profiling-time captures are pure throwaway work, adding startup time proportional to the number of token budgets. Consider profiling only 1-2 budgets (like decoder profile_descs = descs[:2]) instead of capturing all budgets.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time engine startup (profile_cudagraph_memory phase) with many encoder token_budgets configured; compare capturing all budgets vs a 1-2 budget subset for the estimate.

In the per-step decode hot path build(), the new metadata fingerprint reads common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs].numpy() every step. `seq_lens_cpu` is a lazily-materialized (and deprecated) property: `if self._seq_lens_cpu is None: self._seq_lens_cpu = self.seq_lens.to('cpu')` (backend.py:442-445). If this MLA sparse builder path does not otherwise force materialization of seq_lens_cpu, accessing it here triggers a blocking device->host copy on the decode critical path, exactly the H<>D sync the property's deprecation warning cautions against ('Prefer using device seq_lens directly to avoid implicit H<>D sync which breaks full async scheduling'). The parent build() used seq_lens on device only (generate_sparse_seqlen_triton(query_lens, seq_lens, ...)) and never touched seq_lens_cpu, so this diff *introduces* a new per-step D2H readback+sync. The intended savings (skipping get_mla_metadata_v1 launch) may be dwarfed / negated by the forced sync every step, and it also breaks async scheduling overlap.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSv3.2 MI355X TP4 decode-only latency/throughput sweep (batch sizes 1..256), comparing per-step build() latency and end-to-end decode tok/s vs parent; also profile for added hipMemcpyDtoH / stream sync in _prepare_inputs.

findings.claim: The fingerprint also computes np.minimum(seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs].numpy(), topk_tokens) and .tobytes() every step to form metadata_key. Beyond the D2H sync above, this adds per-step host-side array materialization + a byte-serialization keyed dict comparison on the decode critical path. During decode, seq_lens increment by 1 each step, so once seq_lens exceed topk_tokens the clamped values stabilize and the cache hits — but for shorter sequences (< topk_tokens) the key changes every step, making the skip miss and paying both the fingerprint cost AND the original get_mla_metadata_v1 launch (i.e. strictly worse than parent). Worth confirming clamped_seq_lens actually stabilizes for the target workload.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark build() with seq_lens both below and above topk_tokens (2048); measure cache-hit rate of _prev_metadata_key and per-step overhead of tobytes()-keyed comparison.

findings.claim: The CPU sampling path is switched from the PyTorch-native top-k/top-p to the Triton kernel. In apply_top_k_top_p (vllm/v1/sample/ops/topk_topp_sampler.py), the previous 'Keep CPU logits on the PyTorch path to avoid invoking Triton kernels' guard is replaced by: if current_platform.is_cpu(): if HAS_TRITON: return apply_top_k_top_p_triton(logits, k, p). This is exactly a hot-path (per-decode-step sampling) backend swap. If triton-cpu (triton-cpu.git@270e696d, only available when HAS_TRITON) is present in a CPU deployment, every sampling call now routes through the Triton pivot-search kernel instead of the well-optimized native torch sort/topk path. The original comment explicitly warned Triton was avoided on CPU; re-enabling it unconditionally (any batch size, incl. bs<8 that GPU still routes to the PyTorch path) can regress CPU decode latency if the triton-cpu compiled kernel is slower than torch native for small batches / low compute-unit counts.

For single-request / small-batch CPU decode (the common CPU case), the Triton pivot kernel launch + BLOCK_SIZE=256 tiling loop over a 128k vocab may be slower than the native topk-only fast path (apply_top_k_only avoids sorting). Consider mirroring the GPU >=8 gate on CPU.

findings.claim: New default-on PCT auto-binding drastically narrows worker CPU affinity. When numa_bind=True and the user did not pass --numa-bind-cpus (the common case), _get_cpu_binding() now calls _maybe_get_pct_cpu_binding(); on a matching Granite Rapids SKU it replaces the previous full-NUMA-node CPU set (--cpunodebind=<node>, covering all ~64 cores of the node) with --physcpubind=<priority-core subset>, where the filter `cpu_id % stride in (0,1)` keeps only ~8 CPUs per node. This confines each worker's threads (and, via _get_numactl_enginecore_args, the EngineCore) to a tiny fraction of the node's cores. If PCT priority cores do not in fact boost enough to compensate for the >8x reduction in usable CPU threads, this is a throughput/CPU-oversubscription regression relative to the prior full-node binding — an automatic, silent change of *where* work runs. This is exactly the cpu-affinity-numa mechanism (placement/binding of threads) but in the opposite direction (over-restriction rather than missing binding).
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a Xeon 6776P DGX B300, compare end-to-end throughput / CPU-bound preprocessing (tokenization, sampling, host-side scheduling) with numa_bind=True default vs the parent commit's full-node cpunodebind, to verify priority-core restriction does not regress host-side throughput under load.

findings.claim: This PR removes the inplace fused-experts mechanism from the MoE path. In fused_moe.py `fused_experts_impl` the output buffer is now unconditionally `out_hidden_states = torch.empty_like(hidden_states)` (previously `hidden_states if inplace else torch.empty_like(...)`), and likewise `FusedMoEKernelModularImpl.workspace` / marlin_moe output now always allocate a fresh tensor. On torch >= 2.9 this is behavior-preserving: `disable_inplace()` returned True there and the default `FusedMoEConfig.disable_inplace=True`, so `inplace` was already forced False (guarded by `assert not inplace or not disable_inplace()`). The only real regression surface is torch < 2.9 non-DP/EP (TP) MoE layers with no shared experts, where inplace previously reused the activation buffer; those paths now perform one extra activation-sized (`[num_tokens, hidden]`) allocation + write-back per MoE layer forward. This is a hot path (every decode/prefill step), but the allocation is caught by the caching allocator and the extra copy is small relative to the GEMMs, so throughput impact is expected to be minor and only on the older-torch configuration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_moe.py on torch<2.9 comparing TP unquantized/fp8 MoE latency before vs after (inplace previously enabled)

findings.claim: In mhc_post_tilelang the h-block loop was changed from `T.Pipelined(T.ceildiv(h, h_blk), num_stages=2)` to `T.Serial(T.ceildiv(h, h_blk))`. The 2-stage software pipeline previously overlapped the next iteration's global-memory loads (`T.copy(b[i_n,0,i0_h*h_blk], b_shared)` and `T.copy(d[i_n,i0_h*h_blk], d_shared)`) with the current iteration's FMA compute, hiding HBM latency. Serializing the loop makes each iteration wait load->compute->store with no prefetch overlap, exposing global-memory latency on this per-token post kernel. With h_blk=gcd(hidden,1024) the loop typically has several iterations, so lost prefetch is a real latency cost on the hot path (called via torch.ops.vllm.mhc_post_tilelang in mhc.py). This restructures the kernel's latency-hiding scheme rather than merely tuning launch params, matching the kernel-occupancy-redesign mechanism (in the regression direction).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark mhc_post_tilelang latency for representative (num_tokens, hc, hidden) shapes, comparing num_stages=2 pipelined vs the new T.Serial loop to confirm the removed prefetch does not regress throughput.

In silu_mul_quant_fp8_packed_triton the scale buffer is allocated as `torch.zeros((num_packed_groups, tma_aligned_M), int32).T[:M, :]`. The `.T[:M, :]` slices off the TMA padding rows (tma_aligned_M = round_up(M,4)), so the padding region is never written by the kernel — but that padding is exactly what deep_gemm's TMA-aligned scale layout reads. Previously `torch.zeros` guaranteed those padded rows were 0; switching to `torch.empty` leaves the padding uninitialized. The kernel `_silu_mul_quant_fp8_packed_kernel` masks stores with `row_mask = (m_offset+offs_m) < M`, so it writes only the first M rows and never touches the padding. If a downstream TMA-based GEMM (deep_gemm) reads the full tma_aligned_M-row buffer, it will now consume garbage scale bytes. Verify the consumer reads at most M rows before assuming this is safe.
findings.suggested_benchmark: silu_mul_quant_fp8_packed_triton + deep_gemm consumer for M not multiple of 4

findings.claim: This commit reverts `_patch_should_realize_on_reuse` (added in #42686), which patched Inductor's `StorageBox.should_realize_on_reuse` materialization heuristic for torch 2.11.0 (gated by `is_torch_equal("2.11.0")`). Per the reverted patch's own docstring, without this patch Inductor inlines a chain of computations when compiling the whole model graph at once — e.g. the residual in `fused_add_rms_norm` gets recomputed on every use and this recomputation cascades through the model. Reverting it therefore reintroduces redundant recomputation into the compiled model's hot forward path (executed every iteration) on torch 2.11.0 with fullgraph compilation, degrading throughput/latency. torch 2.11 is an actively supported target in this tree (many `is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.11")` gates elsewhere), so this path is live, not dead. Because this is a revert, it may be intentional (the original patch may have caused correctness/compile issues), so confidence is moderate; if 2.11.0 fullgraph compilation is a supported config, a compiled-throughput regression should be expected there.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On torch==2.11.0, compile a model with fused_add_rms_norm using fullgraph/whole-graph Inductor compilation (e.g. Llama-class model) and measure decode throughput and per-forward latency with vs without this revert; inspect the generated Inductor code for recomputed residual/pointwise chains.

findings.claim: Enabling FULL cudagraph capture via TritonMLAMetadataBuilder._cudagraph_support = AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH turns on full-graph capture for the TRITON_MLA decode path, but TritonMLAImpl.forward_mqa is not cudagraph-safe. It (a) allocates fresh intermediate tensors on every call (`o = torch.zeros(...)`, `lse = torch.zeros(...)`, `attn_logits = torch.empty((B, q_num_heads, num_kv_splits, ...))`) whose device pointers change across replays, and (b) derives `num_kv_splits` at runtime from `attn_metadata.max_seq_len` (`ideal_splits = max(1, attn_metadata.max_seq_len // min_work_per_split)`), which then drives the Triton launch grid `grid = (batch, head_num, NUM_KV_SPLITS)`. A captured graph freezes the grid and the intermediate `attn_logits` shape/pointer at capture time; replaying with a different max_seq_len (different num_kv_splits) either produces silently wrong reductions or breaks the static-address assumption. Unlike FlashMLA (which allocates persistent cg_buf_* buffers precisely to be cudagraph-safe), this backend does no such buffer management.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Full-cudagraph decode correctness + latency test on TRITON_MLA across mixed max_seq_len decode batches (e.g. seq_len 512 vs 8192) with cudagraph_mode=FULL; verify outputs match eager and no capture-time errors / illegal grid replays.

The real hot-path concern is the replay path: BreakableCUDAGraphCapture.replay() iterates a Python list of segment callables (`for r in self.segments: r()`) invoking one Python-level call per attention layer per forward. With N decoder layers this replaces the original single monolithic graph.replay() with 2N+1 Python callable dispatches per step. Under decode this adds per-step Python overhead proportional to layer count that the monolithic CUDAGraphWrapper.replay() (single .replay() call) did not have. This is inherent to breakable design but is a latency cost on the hot decode path.

This diff hard-codes get_required_kvcache_layout() to always return "HND" for OffloadingConnector. Via get_kv_connector_cache_layout() in kv_transfer/kv_connector/utils.py, this overrides the default "NHD" attention KV-cache layout for ALL deployments using the OffloadingConnector, regardless of whether the local attention backend actually performs best in HND. HND was originally introduced only for NIXL to accelerate the *transfer* path; forcing it globally on the offloading connector can penalize the *compute* (attention/decode) hot path if the local attention backend is optimized for NHD, converting a per-request savings on offload transfers into a per-forward-step layout penalty. This is a static/default value choosing a potentially slow path for the compute-critical loop, mirroring the config-toggle-perf-feature prior (a fixed config value routing to a non-optimal path).

In fused_indexer_q_rope_quant_mxfp4_cutedsl (called per-forward on the indexer hot path via fused_indexer_q_rope_quant), the function unconditionally loops `for coarsen in (1, 4): IndexerQMxFp4Kernel.compile(...)` on every invocation before then also calling compile() again for the chosen coarsen. Although IndexerQMxFp4Kernel.compile is decorated with @cache so the actual JIT compile only happens once per unique arg tuple, the dispatch still incurs per-call overhead: (a) a Python-level 3x cache lookup / hashing of the (head_dim, rope_dim, num_heads, rope_type, coarsen) key including the CuteDSL type object every forward, and (b) the eager warm-up loop compiles the coarsen=1 AND coarsen=4 variant even when only one is ever used for a given serving shape, doubling first-call JIT latency. The `@cache @staticmethod` stacking (cache above staticmethod) is also fragile — the cache wraps the underlying function object, which works but any change to arg identity (e.g. rope_type being a class each call) matters. This is added CPU-side latency on a per-decode-step path compared to the parent Triton path where triton.jit handles autotune/caching internally.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench fused_indexer_q_rope_quant(use_fp4=True) dispatch overhead per call (num_tokens=1 decode) with cutedsl available: measure host-side latency of the compile() warm-up loop vs a cached single-lookup path.

findings.claim: In CutlassFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.apply_scaled_mm (a per-forward hot path called for every FP8 linear layer via apply_weights), the padding branch executes `B.t().contiguous()` on the weight tensor B every call when K or N is not 16-aligned. This forces a full materialization/copy of the entire [K,N] weight matrix on each forward pass. Since B is a static per-layer weight (constant across all forward calls), this contiguous copy of the weight is redundant work repeated at inference time and could instead be precomputed once in process_weights_after_loading. This is exactly the wasteful layout/copy-per-call mechanism seen historically (SM100 blockwise pad(...).contiguous() copy + post-slice).

findings.claim: The diff flips the static config default `enable_flashinfer_autotune` from True to False for optimization levels O1, O2, and O3 (O2 is the default level). At runtime, kernel_warmup.py checks this flag: when False it logs 'Skipping FlashInfer autotune because it is disabled.' and skips flashinfer_autotune(). Per that function's own docstring, 'Without autotuning, FlashInfer will rely on heuristics, which may be significantly slower.' Thus disabling it forces the slower heuristic-selected FlashInfer kernel path on Hopper (SM 9.0) / Blackwell (SM 10.0) GPUs for all standard optimization levels. This is an intentional performance-for-correctness tradeoff: the commit message and added comment cite flashinfer-ai/flashinfer#3197 correctness issues, so the slower path is deliberately required (not a removable over-restrictive gate). It is a real throughput/latency regression on the affected GPUs, expected to be reverted once the upstream correctness bug is fixed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end decode/prefill throughput on Hopper/Blackwell with a FlashInfer-backed model at -O2, comparing enable_flashinfer_autotune True vs False to quantify the heuristic-vs-autotuned kernel gap.

In the new forward_xpu path (invoked on every decode step's sampling — a hot path), the RNG state is materialized to host and rebuilt via `state = generator.get_state(); seed, offset = state.view(torch.int64); seeds = torch.tensor([seed, offset], dtype=torch.int64, device='cpu')`. `generator.get_state()` returns a device/RNG-backed tensor whose values are pulled to Python scalars when unpacked via `seed, offset = state.view(...)`, forcing a host readback / potential device sync every sampling call. Additionally `torch.tensor([seed, offset], ...)` reconstructs a tensor from Python scalars. This is done unconditionally per invocation, adding a per-step host/device synchronization on the sampling critical path — the same mechanism as the historical blocking-d2h-sync cases (reading a device-side value to host each call).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark forward_xpu vs forward_native over many decode steps on XPU, measuring per-step sampling latency and any host/device sync stalls introduced by generator.get_state() unpacking.

Per Executor.get_class() in vllm/v1/executor/abstract.py (lines 60-67), when distributed_executor_backend=="ray" this env var gates which executor is instantiated: True now selects RayExecutorV2 (MQ/message-queue-based collective_rpc) instead of the previous default RayDistributedExecutor (compiled Ray DAG backend using NCCL/SHM channels). This is the same static-default-toggle mechanism as the historical VLLM_ALLREDUCE_USE_SYMM_MEM cases: a config default silently changes the execution path for every user of the ray backend without an opt-in. The compiled-graph backend was purpose-built for low-latency per-step inter-worker orchestration; the per-decode-step hot path (execute_model / sample_tokens, invoked once per iteration) now routes through the MQ-based RPC of the newly introduced (de5e6c44c) RayExecutorV2. If RayExecutorV2's per-step orchestration latency is higher than the compiled DAG path, this default flip introduces a latency/throughput regression for distributed (TP/PP, multi-node) ray deployments.

The change removes the `shape_invariants` mechanism (`torch._check(input_ids.size()[0] == positions.size()[-1])` etc.) that established equality relations among unbacked symbolic dims, and replaces it with the `shape_id` mechanism (`dynamic_arg_dims={"input_ids": {0: "b"}, "positions": {-1: "b"}, ...}`) that shares one unbacked symbol per shape_id. The updated docstring itself states this relation is needed to 'maximize perf when unbacked shapes are used.' However, `shape_id` is only honored on PyTorch >= 2.11.0 (`_SUPPORTS_SHAPE_ID`). On the still-supported 2.10.x UNBACKED path, the code enters the `is_torch_equal_or_newer('2.10.0')` branch and, because `_SUPPORTS_SHAPE_ID` is False, RAISES RuntimeError for any dim with a shape_id instead of falling back to the old torch._check relations. Net effect on 2.10.x: LlamaModel/Qwen2Model with UNBACKED shapes lose all shape-relation info (no fallback torch._check), so the compiler can no longer prove dims equal — meaning extra/independent unbacked symbols, more data-dependent branching and weaker codegen (the exact perf payoff the invariants provided), and in practice a hard failure. This mirrors the historical 'shape-relation / hint for unbacked symints improves codegen' concern from the kernel-config-tuning hint_override case: the beneficial shape metadata is now version-gated with no equivalent 2.10 path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile + run LlamaModel/Qwen2Model under DynamicShapesType.UNBACKED on torch 2.10.x vs 2.11+; compare compile success, number of unbacked symbols/guards emitted, and generated kernel indexing (32-bit vs 64-bit) and step latency.

findings.claim: This diff FIXES a buffer-sizing perf regression rather than introducing one. Previously the deepstack IntermediateTensors passed the full persistent `_ds_buffers[lvl]` (allocated at max_num_tokens rows) into the compiled Granite4VisionLLMModel. In decode/compiled-CUDA-graph mode `inputs_embeds` has only `n` real tokens (n << max buffer len), so the full-length buffer forced the inner forward's per-layer resize branch (`buf_len != num_tokens` -> torch.nn.functional.pad + slice) to run every layer/every step, and fed a mismatched-shape tensor into the compiled region — the reported decode slowdown. Slicing `[:n]` makes the buffer match `inputs_embeds.size(0)`, so shapes align with the captured graph and the per-layer pad/slice hot-path work is avoided.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-phase latency/throughput for Granite4Vision in compiled (torch.compile + CUDA graph) mode, single-request steady-state decode tokens/s

The new default routes every spec-decode verification step through `probabilistic_rejection_sample`, which launches `_compute_block_stats_kernel` over the full `[num_logits, V]` target logits (per-block max/argmax/sumexp across the whole vocabulary), plus `_probabilistic_rejection_kernel` and `_resample_kernel`. Even in the greedy (`draft_sample_method="greedy"`, `HAS_DRAFT_LOGITS=False`) fast path this still scans the entire vocab per logit every decode step, which is materially more GPU work per verification step than the removed strict kernel. This is a per-iteration hot-path cost increase on the spec-decode critical path introduced purely by the default flip.

findings.claim: When Sequence Parallelism (enable_sp) or async TP (fuse_gemm_comms) is enabled together with piecewise compilation (use_inductor_graph_partition=False and non-empty splitting_ops), this PR now forces full-graph compilation: it sets splitting_ops=[] and, if cudagraph_mode has piecewise cudagraphs (e.g. PIECEWISE or FULL_AND_PIECEWISE), silently downgrades cudagraph_mode to CUDAGraphMode.FULL (see vllm/config/compilation.py new block ~line 1151 and confirmed by tests/compile/test_config.py where PIECEWISE/FULL_AND_PIECEWISE -> FULL, expected_piecewise_compile=False). This static config toggle removes piecewise cudagraphs and piecewise Inductor compilation for the SP-enabled path. For workloads whose batches hit cascade attention or otherwise cannot be fully-captured (FULL cudagraph fallback to eager), the loss of piecewise cudagraphs re-introduces per-step Python/kernel-launch overhead on that fallback path - the same cudagraph-enablement mechanism seen in prior regressions. This is a deliberate SP correctness constraint (SP splits residual across TP ranks, breaking at subgraph boundaries), so it is a correctness-forces-slow-path trade rather than an accidental gate, but users with -O2/-O3 defaults (enable_sp/fuse_gemm_comms via IS_DENSE) on multi-GPU may see reduced cudagraph coverage.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-GPU (TP>=2) dense model with -O2 defaults (enable_sp+fuse_gemm_comms on), compare per-step latency/throughput with cudagraph_mode=FULL_AND_PIECEWISE requested vs the forced FULL, especially for batches that fall into cascade-attention eager fallback.
