findings.claim: The diff introduces an unconditional `torch.accelerator.empty_cache()` call inside `_ensure_workspace_size` on the workspace-growth path. `empty_cache()` is a synchronizing, device-wide operation: it forces a CUDA synchronize and returns all cached-but-unused allocator segments back to the driver. Every subsequent allocation must then go back to `cudaMalloc` (slow, also synchronizing). While workspace growth is supposed to happen only during warmup/profiling and be frozen by lock_workspace(), this call is on the generic get_simultaneous -> _ensure_workspace_size path with no guard beyond `current_size < required_bytes`. If any code path (e.g. elastic-EP unlock_workspace, or a first-touch larger shape after lock isn't yet applied) triggers growth during steady-state execution, each grow now pays a full allocator flush + hard cudaMalloc, defeating the caching allocator and adding a synchronization stall to the hot path. The prior code (torch.empty after del) already relied on the caching allocator reusing the just-freed segment; the added empty_cache() actively destroys that reuse for ALL other cached tensors on the device, not just the freed workspace.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark repeated WorkspaceManager.get_simultaneous with growing sizes (simulating elastic-EP resize / warmup), measuring per-resize latency and steady-state allocator behavior with vs without empty_cache(); also measure device-wide impact by holding unrelated cached tensors and observing re-cudaMalloc cost.

findings.claim: empty_cache() on the accelerator is a heavyweight global operation (device-wide segment reclaim + implicit synchronization). Using it as the mechanism to make room for a single workspace resize is a blunt instrument: it evicts every other unused segment the caching allocator holds, which can force expensive re-allocation of unrelated buffers immediately afterward. A targeted approach (freeing only the old workspace tensor, which the `del current_workspace` already does before torch.empty) would let the allocator reuse the freed segment without a global flush. The claimed benefit (avoiding a dead reserved segment) is real but the cure may cost more than the disease under repeated resizes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak reserved memory AND total allocation latency across a resize sequence with empty_cache() vs relying on del+caching-allocator reuse.

findings.claim: This diff FIXES (does not introduce) a redundant-gate / double-router-invocation regression in Qwen3MoeSparseMoeBlock.forward. Before the change, the block always ran `router_logits, _ = self.gate(hidden_states)` on the host side and then passed those logits into `self.experts(...)`. However, FusedMoE was constructed with `gate=self.gate` (see Qwen3MoeSparseMoeBlock.__init__), which sets `is_internal_router=True`; in that mode FusedMoE runs the gate/router internally again. The result was the gate GEMM being executed twice per MoE layer per forward, and — critically for the historical 'stream-serialization' pattern — the externally-computed gate ran serially before FusedMoE, defeating the intended overlap of the gate/router with the shared-experts / selected-experts path inside FusedMoE. The new code guards on `self.experts.is_internal_router` and, when true, passes `router_logits=hidden_states` directly so the gate runs only once inside FusedMoE (matching the already-fixed qwen3_next.py / deepseek_v2.py / step3p5.py pattern). No new regression is introduced; the diff removes wasted compute and restores the internal-router overlap path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Qwen3-MoE decode/prefill throughput and per-layer MoE latency (nsys trace of the sparse MoE block) comparing before/after; expect one fewer gate GEMM per MoE layer and removal of the serial gate stall.

findings.claim: Enabling VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS by default changes the default KV-cache sizing formula: available_kv_cache_memory_bytes = requested_memory - non_kv_cache_memory - cudagraph_memory_estimate. Previously (flag=0) the CUDA-graph term was 0. The compensating bump of the default gpu_memory_utilization from 0.90 to 0.92 only offsets ~2% of total GPU memory. Where captured CUDA-graph memory exceeds 2% of total device memory (large batch/graph capture sets or large GPUs), the effective default KV-cache pool will SHRINK versus the prior default, reducing max concurrency/throughput for users who relied on the default util. This is the same KV-pool-sizing mechanism as the retrieved prior where changing the gpu_memory_utilization default resized the KV cache.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure num_gpu_blocks / available_kv_cache_memory_bytes at startup with defaults on a large model+GPU (where cudagraph_memory_estimate > 0.02*total) before and after this commit; compare max concurrent sequences and steady-state throughput.

The new parent-side `_startup_prefetch_weights` reads ALL weight shards on 8 threads with no rank/world-size sharding, unlike the existing in-child `_prefetch_all_checkpoints` which shards files across ranks (paths_to_prefetch = sorted_files[rank::world_size]). On single-node multi-GPU (TP>1) the parent reads the FULL weight set into page cache while N EngineCore children then also each try to prefetch their rank's slice. Under memory pressure (weights larger than free RAM, exactly the case where 'prefetch' matters) the parent's full-set read races/contends with and can evict the children's slices, so instead of a head start the two prefetchers thrash the page cache and I/O queue — the same 'duplicated data work' failure mode this category warns about. The parent prefetch does the whole model regardless of TP degree.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cold-page-cache startup TTFT/load-phase timing for a large (>free-RAM) TP=2/4 model: compare load+prefetch wall time with and without parent-side prefetch, plus a case where model weights exceed available RAM.

findings.claim: The diff introduces `@torch.compile(dynamic=True)` on `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize`, which is invoked from the CPU-side multimodal preprocessing path — once per image tile (in `dynamic_preprocess` and `DynamicResolutionImageTiler.apply_params`) and once per video (in `video_to_pixel_values`). torch.compile pays a substantial one-time (init-shaped) tracing/compilation cost on first invocation, and even with `dynamic=True` the guard system frequently re-specializes/re-compiles when input shapes change. Multimodal preprocessing feeds tensors with highly variable (B,C,H,W) shapes (different image resolutions, target sizes, thumbnails vs tiles, per-video frame dims), so the dynamic-shape guards on `F.interpolate(mode='bicubic', antialias=True)` are likely to trigger repeated recompiles across requests. This wraps a memory-bound elementwise+interpolate op (no fusion win to justify the JIT overhead) in a compiled graph on a request-serving preprocessing path, adding latency spikes on cold shapes rather than a steady speedup. This matches the 'init-shaped work wrongly placed on the hot path' boundary of redundant-hot-path-work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench nano_nemotron_vl image+video preprocessing throughput/latency across a mix of image resolutions (e.g. 224,336,448,512, plus odd sizes) and multiple videos; compare eager `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize` vs the `@torch.compile(dynamic=True)` version, measuring first-call compile latency and per-shape recompile count (TORCH_LOGS=recompiles).

In the main-thread hot path (EplbState.step -> the `if self.is_async:` block that runs every forward pass), the parent code only invoked the cross-rank all_reduce (`_all_ranks_buffer_ready`) when `pending_global_ready_check` was True (i.e. gated to only after a rearrangement was triggered). The refactor removes `pending_global_ready_check` and now calls `self._all_ranks_result_ready(eplb_model_state)` for every model_state whenever `eplb_model_state.rebalanced` is True. `_all_ranks_result_ready` performs a collective all_reduce (cpu_group or device_group) on a small int32 flag tensor. Because the condition uses Python short-circuit `rebalanced and self._all_ranks_result_ready(...)`, the all_reduce only fires while a rebalance is in-flight, so it is not literally every step; but during an active async transfer this collective now runs on the critical forward path on every step for every model_state (one per MoE-layer transfer window), whereas before the additional `all_ranks_buffer_ready` gate combined with `ep_buffer_ready` limited how often it ran. This introduces a per-step cross-rank blocking collective on the inference hot path during rebalancing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-latency benchmark (single/short-seq TPOT) with async EPLB enabled and an active rebalance in progress, measuring per-step latency added by the all_reduce in _all_ranks_result_ready.

findings.claim: The new init_cpu_memory_env (csrc/cpu/utils.cpp) performs a page migration/rebinding step that is exactly the class of code that has previously caused CPU-NUMA latency/throughput regressions. It computes the set of nodes to migrate away from as `*(src_mask->maskp) = *(src_mask->maskp) ^ *(mask->maskp)` and then calls `numa_migrate_pages(pid, src_mask, mask)`. The XOR only produces the correct 'source = allowed-minus-target' set when the target mask is a strict subset of the currently-allowed mems; if node_ids contains a node that is NOT in numa_get_mems_allowed(), XOR sets that bit ON in src_mask, so numa_migrate_pages is asked to migrate FROM the destination node (and the destination stays in the source set), producing spurious/failed migrations and potentially churning pages. This mirrors the historical cpu-affinity-numa mechanism (wrong node-set assumption in the migrate/bind path).
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU TP>1 serving throughput/latency with multiple NUMA nodes bound (e.g. VLLM_CPU_SIM_MULTI_NUMA / real 2-socket), compare tok/s and TTFT before vs after.

findings.claim: Every ZeRO-3 allgather now unconditionally enters the SDMA fast-path shim. `_dist_allgather_fn` -> `dist.allgather_fn` -> `TorchBackend.all_gather_into_tensor` now begins with `from . import mori as _mori; sdma_work = _mori.allgather_into_tensor(input_tensor, output_tensor, group=group)` on EVERY call, even in the default (SDMA-off) case which is by far the common path. This adds a module lookup + Python function-call chain (`allgather_into_tensor` -> `supports` -> attribute/dict checks) to the hottest collective in ZeRO-3 (called per parameter bucket, per layer, per forward/backward). The `supports()` short-circuit on `_handle is None` is cheap, but the overhead is non-zero Python work injected into a path that previously went straight to the C++ collective. Consider gating the whole shim behind a single module-level boolean (e.g. `_mori.is_enabled()` cached at TorchBackend init) so SDMA-off runs incur zero per-call cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark N=100k calls to TorchBackend.all_gather_into_tensor with SDMA disabled, before vs after this diff, to quantify per-call Python overhead on the ZeRO-3 prefetch path.

Quantizer now defines __eq__/__hash__ that route through _value_fields() -> _value_key() (a per-field getattr loop building nested tuples plus a _check_value_has_no_process_group() getattr) for every registered value-opaque quantizer (MXFP8/FP8-blockwise/FP8-current-scaling/NVFP4). By the PR's own note these run 'per compiled-function invocation via the EQUALS_MATCH guard', i.e. on the torch.compile guard-evaluation path, and equality/hash on these quantizers previously was O(1) identity. This adds per-invocation Python overhead on the compiled hot path. The PR mitigates it (caches _value_field_names at registration, narrows the process-group check to a single getattr), but the value-key materialization tuple build is still strictly more work than the prior identity semantics.

findings.claim: In DotProductAttention.forward (hot path, executed every attention call), the new _pad_qkv_head_dim runs F.pad on query/key and/or value whenever head_dim_qk != head_dim_v under FA2 MLA. F.pad materializes a fresh, larger contiguous tensor (allocation + zero-fill + copy) for Q, K, and/or V each iteration. Additionally, on the QK>V case, _trim_output does a reshape + slice + reshape after every call. This is a per-iteration copy/allocation cost added to the hot path to work around FA2's lack of native MLA (head_dim_qk != head_dim_v) support. It is a correctness-enabling workaround, not gratuitous, but it does add repeated buffer copies proportional to seqlen*heads on every forward. Consider gating narrowly / documenting that fused/cuDNN backends avoid this padding.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench DotProductAttention.forward with FA2 backend and head_dim_qk != head_dim_v (MLA config, THD layout) vs equal head dims; measure per-call latency and peak memory from the extra F.pad allocations.

findings.claim: The new NVFP4 SReLU backward recompute path breaks the previously-fused activation-recompute + FC2-input-quantization into an extra explicit re-quantization kernel launch. In backward_grouped_mlp.py `_BackwardGroupedMLP_CuTeGEMMDBase.fuser_backward`, the MXFP8 path consumed the columnwise FP4 data/scales directly from the cuDNN dSReLU kernel output (`sfd_col_d_srelu_tensor`) and only assembled a zero-copy GroupedTensor view. The new `use_nvfp4` branch instead materializes the recomputed FC2 input as BF16 (`d_srelu_tensor.view(...).contiguous()`) and then runs a full `_group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp` (columnwise NVFP4 group-quantize) to regenerate FP4 data/scales/amax. This adds a per-backward-step, per-group quantize kernel plus a contiguous() copy of the recomputed activation on the hot training backward path — work that the cuDNN kernel used to fold into its output. This is the inverse of the historical kernel-fusion wins (folding quantization into the GEMM); here quantization is un-fused back out into a separate launch.

findings.claim: The specialized cast-only MXFP8 kernel is now enabled by default (hasSpec<>() changed from `is_cast_only_enabled()`, which read env ENABLE_CAST_ONLY and defaulted to OFF, to unconditional `return true`). This is the *intended* fast-path enablement, but the newly added `scaling_type_has_specialized_support` gate can under-dispatch: (1) COLWISE is dropped from the specialized switch entirely (the `case ScalingType::COLWISE` with the fallback warning was removed) and `scaling_type_has_specialized_support` has no COLWISE clause, so all pure-cast colwise-only requests fall back to the generic (slow) kernel — this matches the historical suboptimal-kernel-gate pattern where a fast kernel exists but gating logic rejects a case. (2) The rowwise clause requires `cols % 128 == 0`; any rowwise tensor whose column count is a multiple of 32 (a valid MXFP8 rowwise block) but not of 128 (e.g. N=32/64/96/160) is forced onto the generic kernel even though it would be perfectly valid to process. Both are legitimate throughput-relevant shapes that silently miss the fast path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark MXFP8 pure-cast quantize (IS_DBIAS/IS_DACT/IS_ACT=false, bf16->fp8e4m3) across shapes: rowwise-only with cols in {32,64,96,128,160,256}; colwise-only; and bidimensional. Compare selected kernel (specialized vs generic) and measured GB/s to confirm which shapes fall back.

findings.claim: In three grouped-linear wgrad allocation paths, `tex.bulk_allocate([weight_shape]*n, [dtype]*n, device, [256]*n)` was replaced by `torch.empty(num_groups, *weight_shape, ...)` followed by Python slicing `[packed[i] for i in range(n)]`. The old `bulk_allocate` explicitly padded each per-expert wgrad tensor to a 256-byte-aligned offset in the backing buffer (see allocate.cpp: `offsets[i]=roundup(base_byte_size,(*alignments)[i])`, alignment=256). The new packed `torch.empty` gives each view an offset of exactly `product(weight_shape)*elementsize` bytes, which is 256-byte aligned only when the per-weight byte size happens to be a multiple of 256. When out_features*in_features*elementsize is not a multiple of 256, per-expert wgrad views become misaligned, which can slow the grouped-GEMM wgrad kernels (worse memory coalescing / disabled vectorized or tensor-core-aligned stores) that these buffers feed into. This is a layout/alignment regression at allocation time, not a copy issue.
findings.suggested_benchmark: GroupedLinear/grouped-MLP backward wgrad microbenchmark across expert weight shapes where out_features*in_features*dtype_bytes is NOT a multiple of 256 (e.g. odd in_features), comparing per-expert wgrad GEMM time before/after; measure aligned vs misaligned view offsets.

findings.claim: Same alignment-loss pattern in the module path: `tex.bulk_allocate([weight_shape]*num_gemms, [activation_dtype]*num_gemms, device, [256]*num_gemms)` replaced with `torch.empty(num_gemms, *weights[0].size(), ...)` and slice views. Views are contiguous-packed with no 256B padding between experts; alignment guarantee for the wgrad GEMM output is lost for non-256B-multiple weight sizes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: _GroupedLinear backward with non-256B-aligned weight byte sizes; compare wgrad GEMM throughput vs parent.

The routing predicate in `_segment_ids_pos_to_seqlens_offsets` was changed to exclude bottom-right causal (BRCM) masks from the O(T) fast path: the parent condition `(attn_mask_type.is_causal() and window_size is None)` matched BRCM (which is_causal() returns True for CAUSAL_BOTTOM_RIGHT_MASK / PADDING_CAUSAL_BOTTOM_RIGHT_MASK), so BRCM with window_size=None previously took the O(T) fast_causal_path. The new condition adds `and not attn_mask_type.is_bottom_right()`, so BRCM now falls through to the new `_get_seqlens_offsets_thd` slow path which is O(T * max_segments_per_seq) per row instead of O(T). This is a per-step (traced-then-executed each iteration) increase in seqlen/offset metadata work for BRCM THD attention. However, this is a required correctness fallback, not an accidental regression: the parent fast path encoded TOP-LEFT causal semantics (segment_pos_q >= segment_pos_kv) which is only correct for BRCM when s_q==s_kv (self-attn); for cross-attention it shifts the bottom-right alignment by one KV per Q row. So the slowdown for BRCM is deliberate to fix incorrect output, and the overall commit still replaces the far worse O(T^2) mask materialization with O(T*max_segments).
findings.suggested_benchmark: THD BRCM (causal_bottom_right) fused attention seqlen/offset metadata microbench comparing parent fast-causal path vs new _get_seqlens_offsets_thd across max_segments_per_seq={1,4,16,64}

findings.claim: Each forward/backward launcher now unconditionally calls check_shared_memory_capacity_num_experts(), which issues cudaGetDevice() + cudaDeviceGetAttribute(cudaDevAttrMaxSharedMemoryPerBlockOptin) plus a cudaFuncSetAttribute() on every kernel launch. These are host-side driver queries executed on the per-launch (hot) path of the fused router; cudaDeviceGetAttribute in particular is not free per-invocation. For workloads that call the router once per layer per step this adds fixed host-side latency to every launch. Consider caching the device attribute (e.g. static/once) and only calling cudaFuncSetAttribute when shared_memory_size actually exceeds the default 48KB static limit, since it is only required for opt-in dynamic shared memory.

findings.claim: This is a correctness bugfix (removing a double all-reduce), not a perf regression. Flipping `output_is_reduced()` from False->True in FlashInferNVLinkOneSided/TwoSided PrepareAndFinalize declares that the combine kernel (mnnvl_moe_alltoallv_combine) already reduces the fused output. In moe_runner.py, `_fused_output_is_reduced` gates `_maybe_reduce_final_output`: with the previous `False`, a `tensor_model_parallel_all_reduce(states)` was performed on the combined output even though the MNNVL combine already summed contributions across ranks — a redundant/extra collective (double reduce). Setting it to `True` skips that late all-reduce, REMOVING a communication op. Therefore the diff reduces, not increases, comm overhead and does not match the ipc-transport-overhead / missing-comm-overlap patterns as an introduced regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE EP throughput/latency with FlashInfer NVLink one-sided and two-sided backends (TP>1 or EP>1) to confirm the removed all-reduce doesn't harm correctness and improves latency

findings.claim: This PR bumps the pinned TransformerEngine external git ref from 2.14.0 (f031cf87) to 2.15.0 (42b84005) in pyproject.toml/uv.lock. TE supplies the fused/FP8 attention+MLP kernels and CUDA-graph-capturable paths on the hot inference/training path. Bumping the pinned kernel-backend ref is exactly the historical 'fused-backend-swap / kernel-config-tuning' mechanism where a newer upstream commit can change kernel selection/heuristics and regress throughput or latency. Corroborating signal: in the SAME PR the FP8 CUDA-graph static-inference test `gpt_static_inference_tp1_pp1_583m_fp8_cudagraphs` was quarantined — its scope moved from active [mr, mr-github] to [mr-broken, mr-github-broken] (tests/test_utils/recipes/h100/gpt-static-inference.yaml). Quarantining the FP8+cudagraph test alongside the TE bump strongly indicates the new TE 2.15 broke or regressed the FP8 CUDA-graph inference fast path rather than the bump being purely cosmetic.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Re-enable and run gpt_static_inference_tp1_pp1_583m_fp8_cudagraphs on dgx_h100 with TE 2.14 vs 2.15; compare decode tokens/s and per-step latency for the FP8 + CUDA-graph path.

clip_qk() in megatron/core/optimizer/qk_clip.py issues one torch.distributed.all_reduce(op=MAX) over the data-parallel(+CP) group PER transformer layer PER optimizer step (loop over model_chunk.decoder.layers). This is a per-layer collective rather than a single batched all_reduce over a stacked tensor of all layers' current_max_attn_logits. For deep models this is O(num_layers) small collectives on the optimizer step critical path, each with its own latency/launch overhead. Batching all layers' logits into one tensor and doing a single all_reduce would reduce collective count. Matches the collective-payload/redundant-collective family (many tiny collectives).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time train_step optimizer phase with --qk-clip on a 64-layer model at high DP world size; compare per-layer all_reduce vs a single batched all_reduce.

The `_A_neg_exp_cache` is unconditionally re-copied (`copy_(-torch.exp(...))`) on EVERY decode call whenever `torch.cuda.is_current_stream_capturing()` is True. During piecewise/per-layer CUDA-graph capture this means the exp+neg+copy is baked into the captured graph and thus re-executed on every replay too, so the cache provides no benefit under CUDA-graph replay and adds the exact recompute it aims to avoid. The stale-flag optimization only helps in the eager (non-captured) decode path. Verify whether the graph-replay decode path (the common hot path with cuda graphs enabled) actually benefits.

These recipe objects and the amax group are effectively static per-module and could be constructed once at finish_init and cached, rather than reconstructed per forward per microbatch. Reconstructing recipe objects and re-querying the parallel-state amax group in the hot path is wasted per-step work that scales with #matched-layers * #microbatches. This resembles the config-toggle-perf-feature pattern where a precision/recipe path is set up repeatedly instead of once.

findings.claim: The new FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel is inserted at the TOP of _POSSIBLE_NVFP4_KERNELS[CUDA] (before FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel), making it the new default (auto-selected) NVFP4 linear kernel on SM100. But unlike FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel, the CuteDSL kernel does NOT override input_quant_key() (it stays None from the base class). That method is exactly the hook that expose_input_quant_key()/as_quantized_activation() use to let fusion passes fuse the activation nvfp4-quant into an upstream op and hand the kernel a pre-quantized activation. Because the CuteDSL kernel returns None, as_quantized_activation() cannot short-circuit, so every apply_weights() call unconditionally re-runs scaled_fp4_quant(x, ...) on the hot path. Making this the auto default silently disables the fused-activation-quant fast path that the previous default (cutlass) enabled, adding a per-forward quant kernel launch for every NVFP4 linear layer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On SM100, run a modelopt/compressed-tensors NVFP4 model end-to-end with --linear-backend auto vs flashinfer_cutlass and compare decode/prefill latency; also compare against explicitly forcing flashinfer_cutedsl to confirm the fused quant path (input_quant_key=kNvfp4Dynamic) is missing.

findings.claim: This is a fused-backend-swap: the auto-selection default for NVFP4 linear GEMM on CUDA/SM100 changes from FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel to FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel by inserting the new kernel first in the priority list. Unlike b12x (explicitly commented as excluded from auto-selection and opt-in only), the CuteDSL backend is enabled by default. If CuteDSL is not universally faster than cutlass across shapes/batch sizes, this changes the default hot-path GEMM for all SM100 NVFP4 deployments without an autotune/heuristic guard.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep NVFP4 GEMM shapes (M from 1..8192, common N/K for target models) comparing cute-dsl vs cutlass flashinfer_scaled_fp4_mm latency to confirm cute-dsl is >= cutlass everywhere it is now auto-selected.

In SingleTypeKVCacheManager.allocate_new_blocks and allocate_new_computed_blocks, the new `self.new_block_ids.extend(b.block_id for b in ...)` is executed on every block allocation (a hot path called each scheduling step) whenever `type(self.kv_cache_spec) is FullAttentionSpec`. This is guarded only by the spec-type check, NOT by whether the model actually needs zeroing. The scheduler only calls `kv_cache_manager.take_new_block_ids()` (which drains the list) when `self.needs_kv_cache_zeroing` is True, and `needs_kv_cache_zeroing == has_mamba_layers`. For the common case of pure full-attention models (no Mamba), `take_new_block_ids()` is never called, so the per-allocation extend()+generator work runs every step for no benefit, and worse, `self.new_block_ids` grows unbounded (never drained) for the lifetime of the process — a memory leak proportional to total blocks ever allocated.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a long-lived pure full-attention model (no Mamba), monitor resident memory of the scheduler process over many thousands of steps; also microbenchmark allocate_new_blocks throughput with vs without the unconditional new_block_ids.extend.

findings.claim: Pinning is now performed on a background thread (_pin_cpu_tensors) and the pin_thread is only joined at shutdown, not before the handlers start serving transfers. transfer_async()/submit_store()/submit_load() can be called by the connector worker while the background thread has not yet finished cudaHostRegister on the CPU tensors (which can take seconds for multi-GB regions per the logged timing). During that window the H2D/D2H swap_blocks copies run against still-unpinned host memory, i.e. slower pageable DMA. This is a transient (bounded-at-startup) latency regression on the offload transfer hot path, not a monotonic leak. It is the intended trade-off of the PR (unblock init) but there is no synchronization ensuring the first transfers actually see pinned memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU->CPU / CPU->GPU KV offload transfer throughput for requests issued in the first few seconds after worker init vs steady state; verify no sustained slowdown from unpinned DMA.

findings.claim: In UBatchWrapper.__call__ (gpu_ubatch_wrapper.py), the total token count used as the cudagraph cache key is still computed as num_tokens = (ubatch_slices[0].token_slice.stop - ubatch_slices[0].token_slice.start) * 2 — hardcoded to 2 ubatches. The PR generalizes DBO->XBO with parallel_config.num_ubatches (via --ubatch-size), but this key was NOT updated to multiply by num_ubatches. For any N-way (num_ubatches > 2) XBO run, this key equals only 2/N of the real token count, so it will (a) collide with / mis-key entries in self.cudagraphs and (b) mismatch the num_tokens value used elsewhere. This defeats cudagraph reuse for the new XBO path — every step falls into the capture/replay-miss branch, forcing recapture or an eager (non-graph) run, i.e. the overlap-with-graph fast path silently doesn't fire for N>2. Should be * num_ubatches (or sum over ubatch_slices).

findings.claim: New MergedColumnParallelLinearWithLoRA.apply() routes the non-fully-sharded merged-column LoRA path through _mcp_apply(), which performs a tensor_model_parallel_all_gather() on every forward. In the parent, base MergedColumnParallelLinearWithLoRA had NO apply() override and inherited BaseLinearLayerWithLoRA.apply -> _apply_sync (punica add_lora_linear, no collective). For a plain MergedColumnParallelLinear base layer with TP>1, the new dispatch (`self.tp_size == 1 and type(base) is not merged_cls ...` is False, so it falls to `return _mcp_apply(x, bias, self)`) now issues an extra all-gather collective per LoRA merged-column layer per forward step. This is the classic 'redundant/extra collective introduced on a hot per-layer path' regression: LoRA merged-column layers under TP now pay an all-gather they previously avoided.

When VLLM_LORA_ENABLE_DUAL_STREAM is on, these layers previously overlapped the base GEMM with the LoRA compute on a separate CUDA stream; now they run base+LoRA sequentially with no overlap, adding a stream-serialization bubble on the hot forward path for Replicated and merged-column LoRA layers.

findings.claim: The NCCL_MAX_CTAS override in override_envs_for_eplb (eplb_utils.py) is now gated on is_nccl_based_eplb_communicator (communicator in {'torch_nccl','pynccl'}). However, the new auto-select default in parallel.py sets communicator='torch_gloo' whenever use_async is enabled. Since torch_gloo is NOT in the nccl-based set, the NCCL_MAX_CTAS=8 SM-margin reservation is now SKIPPED for the common async EPLB + deepep_low_latency + data_parallel case (previously it was applied whenever async_eplb was true). The original deadlock guard mitigated a NCCL vs DeepEP LM SM contention hang; with gloo weight-exchange there is no NCCL EPLB traffic so the guard is arguably unnecessary, but this changes behavior: the default async path silently switches from NCCL P2P weight exchange (device-to-device) to gloo CPU-staged exchange, which adds D2H+H2D copies and a full CUDA stream synchronize per transfer round in TorchDistGlooStagedEplbCommunicator.execute(). This is a slower data path chosen by a static default value.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Async EPLB weight-exchange latency: torch_gloo (CPU-staged, new default) vs torch_nccl (device P2P) on a MoE model with deepep_low_latency, measuring per-rebalance transfer time and steady-state decode throughput.

findings.claim: In `_gumbel_sample_kernel`, `logits = logits.to(tl.float64)` widens the entire per-block vocab vector (BLOCK_SIZE=1024 per program, tiled over the full vocab_size) to FP64, so the temperature division (`logits / temp`), the gumbel addition (`logits + gumbel_noise`), and the block-wide reduction (`tl.max`) now execute in FP64. This kernel runs on every decode step for every sampled token across the full [num_tokens, vocab_size] logits tensor — a hot sampling path. FP64 ALU throughput is 1/2 (datacenter A100/H100) to ~1/32-1/64 (consumer Ada/Ampere) of FP32, so this vocab-wide FP64 arithmetic + reduction can materially increase sampling latency vs the previous FP32 path (which this reverses; cf. prior commit #34854 'Use FP32 for Gumbel Noise'). Only the final gumbel `u` computation actually needs FP64 precision; keeping the logits/temperature/reduction in FP32 and adding FP64 noise only where numerically required would avoid the vocab-wide FP64 cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark gumbel_sample over vocab_size in {32k,128k,256k} with num_tokens sweep, comparing FP32-logits vs FP64-logits kernel latency on both a datacenter (H100) and consumer GPU; measure end-to-end decode-step sampling latency.

The parent already had a finer-grained mechanism: KVConnectorFactory.create_connector checks `hma_enabled = not disable_hybrid_kv_cache_manager` and `supports_hma(connector_cls)`, and the PR #25712 ('Enable hybrid allocator + KV cache connector') deliberately allowed HMA to stay ON for connectors implementing SupportsHMA. This diff regresses that by blanket-disabling HMA for every connector regardless of `supports_hma`, so even HMA-capable connectors now fall back to the uniform allocator. The change over-broadens the disable condition (the code comment itself flags this as a temporary TODO), so previously-fast HMA-enabled connector paths are now unnecessarily slowed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an HMA-supporting connector on a hybrid-attention model and confirm HMA no longer engages after this commit (block-count / memory-utilization and TTFT/throughput delta vs parent).

findings.claim: This diff unconditionally force-sets `scheduler_config.disable_hybrid_kv_cache_manager = True` whenever `self.kv_transfer_config is not None` (vllm/config/vllm.py, new block after line ~597). This routes ALL deployments using a KV connector (NIXL, LMCache, shared-storage, CPU offload, MultiConnector) onto the non-hybrid/uniform KV cache allocator, disabling HMA. HMA gives right-sized, per-attention-type KV block allocation for models with sliding-window or Mamba attention — turning it off reduces achievable KV capacity (fewer concurrent blocks) and throughput for those models. This is a static config toggle choosing the slow path, matching the historical `config-toggle-perf-feature` pattern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput/max-concurrency for a sliding-window (e.g. Gemma-2/Ministral) or Mamba model run with `--kv-transfer-config` set to an HMA-capable connector, comparing this commit vs parent (which kept HMA enabled when the connector supported it).

The new execute_in_parallel() allocates aux-GEMM output tensors (kv_score, indexer_weights, indexer_kv_score) INSIDE the aux-stream context (allocation happens when fn() runs under `with torch.cuda.stream(aux_streams[i])`), but these tensors are subsequently consumed on the default stream (kv_score -> compressor(kv_score,...) on default; indexer_weights/indexer_kv_score passed into indexer()/fused_indexer_q_rope_quant). Cross-stream synchronization is done only via done_events[i].wait() on the default stream (ordering), NOT via tensor.record_stream() on the caching allocator. Because the CUDA caching allocator tracks the *allocating* stream, once the default stream stops referencing the tensor the block can be freed and reused while the aux stream (or a later realloc) still logically owns it — the exact record_stream-missing race pattern seen in the shared-experts dual-stream overlap (#26440). Add tensor.record_stream(current_stream) for each aux result before returning, or allocate the output on the default/consumer stream.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSV4 decode/prefill correctness + throughput under high concurrency with memory pressure (forces allocator block reuse); compare vs parent to detect nondeterministic outputs.

findings.claim: init_cpu_memory_env selects numa_set_membind(mask) for a single node and numa_set_interleave_mask(mask) plus numa_set_strict(1) for multiple nodes. numa_set_strict(1) makes membind/interleave allocation strict — allocations that cannot be satisfied on the bound node(s) fail rather than falling back to another node. If the chosen node set does not actually contain enough free memory for the KV cache / model (note get_device_total_memory now reports only a single node's MemTotal via get_memory_node_info(device_id)), strict binding can cause OOM-style failures or heavy reclaim/thrash, degrading throughput/latency. This is the same interleave-vs-membind placement mechanism flagged historically.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run TP>1 on a multi-NUMA host and compare throughput/latency with numa_set_strict(1) enabled vs default policy; also test single-node sizing where per-node MemTotal underestimates available cache space.

The new `_startup_prefetch_weights` in api_server.py unconditionally reads ALL weight shards (*.safetensors, *.bin) fully into page cache from the parent APIServer process, but it bypasses every guard the existing in-child prefetch (`_prefetch_all_checkpoints`/`maybe_prefetch_checkpoints` in weight_utils.py) uses to decide *whether* prefetching is beneficial: (1) no `should_prefetch`/safetensors_load_strategy gate, (2) no filesystem-type check (the in-child path disables prefetch on non-local/network FS), (3) no `fits_in_ram` check, and (4) no rank/world_size sharding (`sorted_files[rank::world_size]`). Reading the entire weight set (tens of GB) with 8 threads from the parent while the child later reads the same files means on RAM-constrained or memory-tight hosts the parent-warmed pages can be evicted before the child faults on them, and on network/non-seekable filesystems the extra full read is pure duplicated I/O with no page-cache benefit — a net startup slowdown and I/O contention rather than the intended speedup.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cold-cache `vllm serve` startup time on (a) a large (>host-RAM-fraction) model on local NVMe and (b) a model on a network filesystem, comparing wall-clock to first-token vs parent commit; also measure peak page-cache pressure / eviction.

findings.claim: The parent-side prefetch reads full weight bytes with `while f.read(block_size): pass` across 8 ThreadPoolExecutor workers concurrently with the engine child's own in-child prefetch (which also uses 8 threads). Both run against the same files at roughly overlapping times; without coordination this doubles the effective read-thread count against the storage device and can cause queue-depth thrashing that slows, not speeds, the load phase — the exact I/O-contention scenario the in-child prefetch's rank-sharding was designed to avoid.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure aggregate disk read throughput and load-phase duration with parent+child prefetch both active vs child-only, on a single-queue disk.

PackedSeqParams.__post_init__ now unconditionally computes seq_idx (torch.cat + repeat_interleave + arange + to(int32) + unsqueeze) whenever total_tokens is set and cu_seqlens is a Tensor. Previously _create_packed_seq_idx was only invoked inside MambaMixer when sequence packing was active. Now every PackedSeqParams construction with total_tokens (e.g. RL get_logprobs building a single-sequence default per call, pretrain_mamba forward_step) eagerly launches these small GPU ops even for non-Mamba/non-packed usage where seq_idx is never consumed (e.g. TE attention path where total_tokens/seq_idx are explicitly discarded). This adds small per-construction GPU kernel launches on hot RL/train paths. Consider lazy computation of seq_idx only when consumed by the Mamba mixer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench PackedSeqParams construction overhead (with total_tokens set) per training/inference step, and count of unused seq_idx computations on the TE-attention-only path.

findings.claim: register_z3_param now performs a full ProcessGroup::allgather + blocking wait() for EVERY parameter at registration time, purely to validate that padded shard sizes are uniform across ranks. init_z3.py calls dc.register_z3_param() in a loop over ALL model parameters (for p in engine.module.parameters()), so this adds one collective per parameter during initialization. For large models with thousands of parameters this serializes O(num_params) synchronous allgather+wait+device-to-CPU (.item()) round trips at startup. Each iteration also does .to(torch::kCPU).item<int64_t>() per rank (world_size synchronous D2H copies), which is a classic latency killer. Because it is a collective inside a per-parameter loop it must run identically on all ranks; any divergence in the parameter iteration order across ranks would hang.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure init_z3() wall time (model registration phase) for a large model (e.g. >1000 params) with world_size>=2 before/after this commit; expect O(num_params) added collective+D2H latency.

findings.claim: The gathered output buffer is now allocated at padded_numel = world_size * padded_per_rank (rounded up) instead of the exact param.getShape() size. For persistent/gathered params this permanently enlarges every gathered-param buffer, and every allgatherParam / prefetchParamsFused call also allocates the larger padded buffer. The returned tensor is a sliced view (.flatten().index(Slice(0,true_numel)).view(shape)) of the padded buffer, so the whole padded allocation is kept alive as long as the view is referenced, increasing resident memory for all ZeRO-3 gathered params.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak GPU memory for a ZeRO-3 DeepCompile run with parameters whose numel is not divisible by world_size, before/after the change.

This PR is itself a rework of the internal-DP load balancing routing that the retrieved historical cases describe (matched_leaf: scheduler-work-distribution). The two flagged mechanisms are present in the diff as INTENDED FIXES, not regressions: (1) In DPLBAsyncMPClient.__init__, eng_start_index = (len(self.core_engines) * self.client_index)//client_count now spreads each front-end client's start offset across engines instead of all clients starting at client_index % num_engines — this improves balancing across multiple API-server processes. (2) get_core_engine_for_request switches from a lexicographic [waiting,running] comparison to a scalar score = waiting*4 + running with a scalar min_score, and adds current_counts[eng_index][0] += self.client_count as a local optimistic-load adjustment. These changes reduce, not increase, the routing-imbalance (gpu-util-or-bubble) risk. No regression introduced here.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-client internal-DP throughput/latency test: run N front-end API servers against DP>2 engines under skewed request arrival and measure per-engine occupancy variance and p99 TTFT vs parent.

findings.claim: This diff intentionally disables the cross-layer (unified/contiguous) KV cache layout for MLA and DeepseekV32Indexer backends. By making get_kv_cache_stride_order(include_num_layers_dimension=True) return the identity permutation (0,1,2,3) (previously (1,0,2,3) for MLA), combined with the new check in use_uniform_kv_cache() (`kv_cache_stride_order[0] != 0`), MLA layers will now fall back to per-layer KV cache tensors. The cross-layer layout coalesced per-block KV data for all layers into a single contiguous buffer so KV transfer / swap_blocks could move all layers at once with one transfer; disabling it means per-layer transfers again (the historical [buffer-sizing-layout] latency mechanism). This is a deliberate correctness fix (MLA kernels require contiguous per-layer views, so the cross-layer layout would produce wrong results), so it is a correctness-forced reversion of an optimization rather than an accidental regression. Net effect: MLA + KV-connector (NIXL/offloading disaggregated prefill/decode) paths lose the single-transfer optimization and incur per-layer transfer overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Disaggregated prefill/decode KV-transfer throughput/latency for a DeepSeek MLA model with NIXL or offloading connector, comparing per-layer vs cross-layer transfer time.

findings.claim: In `_apply_rotary_pos_emb_thd`, CASE 2 (max-seqlen freqs, the legacy/common path) is restructured back into a per-sequence loop that calls `_apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd` once for every packed sequence (plus an `output.narrow(...).copy_()` per sequence). The parent code built a single packed `freqs_packed` via cheap freq-slice concatenation and then invoked `_apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd` exactly ONCE on the whole packed tensor. Since each `_apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd` call launches multiple elementwise/allocation kernels (torch.cos, torch.sin, _rotate_half's chunk+cat, and a final torch.cat), moving it inside the loop multiplies all those kernel launches and intermediate-tensor allocations by the number of packed sequences (batch size), and adds an extra per-sequence copy. This reintroduces exactly the throughput regression the historical THD-RoPE case was fixed to avoid (consolidating per-sequence applies into one packed apply). This is a hot path executed every forward step for packed-sequence/THD (e.g. VLM) training.

findings.claim: `_has_module` previously only ran `importlib.util.find_spec` (a metadata/on-disk lookup that does NOT execute module top-level code). The diff now additionally runs `importlib.import_module(module_name)` on the first (uncached) call, actually importing and executing the target module. For heavyweight optional deps checked at startup/first-use — e.g. `has_deep_gemm()` (imports `deep_gemm`, which may JIT-compile / load native CUDA libs), `has_deep_ep()`, `has_triton_kernels()` (triton_kernels does 'additional side effects' per LazyLoader docstring), `has_aiter`, `has_fbgemm_gpu`, `has_cutedsl('cutlass')`, `has_tilelang` — the probe now forces full module initialization plus dynamic-library loading rather than a cheap existence check. This converts a cheap capability query into an eager import with potential compile/load side effects, adding startup/first-call latency even when the dependency is installed. Behavior is arguably correct (verifies native deps), but the perf cost per unique module is real; it is bounded by @cache to one import per module name.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure cold-start time of has_deep_gemm()/has_triton_kernels()/has_aiter() with the dependency installed, before vs after this change, to quantify the added import + native-lib-load cost.

findings.claim: The shared-expert intermediate_size is rounded up (cdiv) to a TP-uniform, block-aligned size when weight_block_size is set and not sequence-parallel. This padding is not merely a loader convenience: it changes the shape passed to MergedColumnParallelLinear/RowParallelLinear at construction, so the shared-expert gate_up/down GEMMs run on the padded (zero-filled) intermediate dimension for the entire lifetime of the model, not just at load time. For TEP=16 with a moderate moe_intermediate_size the rounding can add a full extra 128-block on trailing ranks, i.e. a persistent per-token FLOP/bandwidth overhead in the shared-expert MLP on every forward pass. This is a genuine tradeoff (alignment/correctness vs. wasted steady-state compute) rather than a bug; worth confirming the pad fraction is small (typically <=1 block per rank) so the extra shared-expert GEMM cost stays negligible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark DeepSeek-V4 block-FP8 shared-expert MLP forward latency at TEP=16 vs a non-padded baseline (or a TP size that divides evenly), measuring per-token shared-expert GEMM time to quantify the padding overhead.

In serve_tokens_stream_generator (streaming/DELTA path), the diff adds per-delta base64 serialization of output.routed_experts: for every streamed chunk it allocates an io.BytesIO, runs np.save (full .npy header + array serialization) and base64-encodes the result. Per output_processor.py:398-400, routed_experts is only populated at finish (routed_experts = None until `finished`, then np.concatenate over chunks). Therefore, in DELTA streaming mode this new block does non-trivial work only on the final chunk — but crucially it serializes the FULL cumulative [seq_len, layer_num, topk] array of the entire generation on that final streaming chunk, i.e. an O(total_tokens * layers * topk) numpy+base64 encode executed inside the token-streaming loop rather than off the critical decode path. For long generations with many MoE layers this is a sizeable synchronous CPU cost embedded in the streaming generator, blocking the event loop right at end-of-stream.

findings.claim: `_clear_mm_prefix_for_full_attn_layers()` is invoked on the per-forward hot path (inside the model forward, outside the compiled boundary) and iterates over the entire per-layer attn_metadata dict on every forward step. For each layer it does a Python string parse (`layer_name.split('.layers.')[1].split('.')[0]` + `int()`) plus `hasattr` checks. This repeats every decode/prefill iteration for all layers, even though the layer->index mapping is static. The commit correctly precomputed `_full_attn_layer_idxs` in __init__ (avoiding per-call regex/config parsing), but the string parsing of layer_name and the O(num_layers) walk still runs every step. Consider precomputing the set of matching metadata keys once (or caching the layer_name->idx parse) to avoid repeated string splitting on the hot path. Impact is bounded (only for Gemma4 bidi='vision' models) and guarded by an early return when `_full_attn_layer_idxs` is empty, so it is a minor concern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-latency benchmark for a Gemma4 model with use_bidirectional_attention='vision' and many layers, comparing per-step overhead of the metadata walk.

findings.claim: This diff removes `fastsafetensors @ git+...` (the '# Required for faster safetensors model loading' entry) from requirements/rocm.txt, i.e. from the ROCm *runtime* dependency set. fastsafetensors is the GPU-accelerated (GPU-direct-storage) safetensors loader selected via `--load-format fastsafetensors`; when it is not installed, `vllm/model_executor/model_loader/weight_utils.py` falls back to a PlaceholderModule and `default_loader.py::_get_weights_iterator` cannot take the fast path (`fastsafetensors_weights_iterator`). This is the exact inverse of the historical 'enable fastsafetensors on ROCm' change — ROCm users who relied on this fast loader will lose the accelerated startup/weight-load path (or hit a placeholder import error), raising model startup/weight-load time. Note this is opt-in (default load-format is auto/hf), so it is NOT a regression for default users; the motivation (uv cannot resolve git+ URLs, and PyPI only ships CUDA wheels) is a legitimate packaging fix. Consider re-adding via a PyPI-published ROCm wheel or an opt-in extra so the fast loader stays available on ROCm.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a ROCm host, compare model weight-load time (the 'Loading weights took X seconds' log in default_loader.load_weights) with `--load-format fastsafetensors` vs default `auto`, before and after this commit, to confirm the fast path is still available.

findings.claim: The diff adds a Python-level `@model_serializer(mode="wrap")` callback (`_serialize`) to `DeltaMessage` (vllm/entrypoints/openai/engine/protocol.py) and `ChatMessage` (vllm/entrypoints/openai/chat_completion/protocol.py). `DeltaMessage` is serialized via `model_dump_json(exclude_unset=True)` in the streaming hot path of chat_completion_stream_generator (serving.py lines ~533, ~569, ~751) — i.e. once per streamed delta chunk / per token. A custom wrap serializer forces pydantic-core to invoke this Python function (calling `handler(self)`, building a dict, `data.get(...)`, `data.pop(...)`) on every chunk instead of taking the pure-Rust fast serialization path. For high-token-throughput streaming this adds a small per-chunk Python overhead that scales with the number of streamed tokens. The alternative (`exclude_defaults`/a field-level default that OpenAI-clients treat as None) would avoid the per-chunk Python callback. Note this mirrors an existing precedent (`ChatCompletionToolsParam._serialize`) which runs only per-request, not per-token, so the hot-path exposure is new here.

findings.claim: extract_tool_calls_streaming() is the per-token streaming hot path (invoked once for every generated token). The new MiniCPM5XMLToolParser rebuilds all tool lookup maps from scratch on every streamed delta: _process_complete_block_streaming() and _process_partial_block_streaming() each call _build_tool_maps(request.tools), which iterates the full tools list and constructs four dicts/sets (name_to_tool, name_to_allowed_props, name_to_required, name_to_tool) every invocation. These maps depend only on request.tools (constant across a request) and could be computed once (e.g. cached on the parser instance keyed by request identity, similar to qwen3xml's set_tools() pattern) instead of per token. This is exactly the 'repeats every iteration' shape rather than one-time setup, and grows O(#tools * #properties) per token.

findings.claim: The final vLLM runtime image previously set `ENV OMP_NUM_THREADS=16` (docker/Dockerfile.ppc64le:289 at parent). The rewritten final stage in this PR drops that ENV. For the CPU/ppc64le backend, OpenMP thread count directly governs matmul/attention throughput. With OMP_NUM_THREADS unset, at runtime vLLM/torch falls back to a heuristic default (multiproc_executor.py sets a computed default when OMP_NUM_THREADS not in os.environ, and torch_utils.py logs 'OMP_NUM_THREADS is not set; defaulting Torch threads to %d'). On large SMT Power hosts this fallback can either over-subscribe cores (thread contention) or under-utilize them relative to the previously curated value of 16, changing steady-state CPU inference throughput. This is a static-default/config change (a shipped env default removed), matching the config-toggle-perf-feature mechanism (default now selects a non-tuned thread count). Worth confirming the intended CPU thread default for the new prebuilt-wheel path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU decode/prefill throughput (tokens/s) on a Power9/Power10 host comparing image with OMP_NUM_THREADS=16 vs unset default, same core count

findings.claim: The new `_get_modelinfo_module_hash` replaces reading a single file (`open(model_path).read()`) with, for package entry points (`__init__.py`), a recursive `model_path.parent.rglob('*.py')` glob plus a `sorted(...)` and reading every submodule's bytes into the hasher. This is used to compute the cache key BEFORE consulting the on-disk model-info cache in `inspect_model_cls`. For any model registered via a package `__init__.py`, this walks & fully reads the entire package's Python source tree on every inspection call, whereas previously it read exactly one file. Since this runs before the cache lookup, the extra directory-walk/read cost is paid even on cache hits. This is startup/load-time work (model registry inspection), not per-token hot-path, so blast radius is bounded to init; but it does grow with package size and is unconditional. Consider caching the computed module_hash per _LazyRegisteredModel instance to avoid re-globbing on repeated inspect calls.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure ModelRegistry.inspect_model_cls / _try_inspect_model_cls latency (and vLLM engine startup time) for a model whose registry entry points at a package __init__.py with many submodules, comparing single-file read vs rglob-of-all-*.py.

findings.claim: The model instantiates one distinct Attention instance (and thus one distinct KV cache slot / layer_name) per recurrence step: H stack has H_cycles instances and L stack has H_cycles*L_cycles instances per layer_idx_in_stack. Every one of these gets its own FullAttentionSpec KV cache allocation, so the total KV cache footprint scales with the number of recurrence steps, not the number of physical weight-sharing layers. This is required by the recurrent design but dramatically increases KV memory (and reduces max concurrency / available blocks) versus a normal decoder of equivalent parameter count. Reviewers should ensure users are aware KV capacity is multiplied by the recurrence-step count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare reported num_gpu_blocks / kv_cache_size_tokens for HRM-Text vs a same-parameter causal decoder to quantify the KV-cache multiplier from per-step KV slots.

findings.claim: In the output dispatcher hot loop (`run_output_dispatcher_loop`), `inner.lora_adapter_states()` is now called unconditionally on every scheduler-stats-bearing output batch (roughly every engine/scheduler tick). It takes the `request_reg` lock and iterates the ENTIRE active-requests HashMap, and for each LoRA request `clone()`s its adapter-name String into a freshly allocated `BTreeSet` — every step, even when the running/waiting adapter membership is unchanged. Only after building the two sets does `LoraInfoExporter::update` diff them. This is O(active_requests) iteration + per-step String-clone/BTreeSet-allocation churn on the highest-frequency path, wasted on the common case where the label set is stable step-to-step (and, for non-LoRA deployments, still an O(active_requests) walk plus the lock hold on every tick). Mechanistically this mirrors the historical 'rebuild the whole collection every step regardless of what changed' pattern (quadratic-python-structure leaf), though here the cost is linear, not quadratic.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Steady-state decode with N concurrent LoRA requests (e.g. N=256) across several adapters; measure dispatcher-loop CPU / per-step latency and lock hold time on the request registry with vs without the lora_adapter_states() snapshot per tick.

findings.claim: The new emit_cached_block_events() branch runs on the per-request scheduler path (invoked from get_computed_blocks() -> right after find_longest_cache_hit) whenever kv_cache_report_mode == 'full' and KV events are enabled. For each prefix-cache hit it re-derives block_hashes via resolve_block_hashes(), then loops over every cached block calling generate_block_hash_extra_keys() and maybe_convert_block_hash(), and slices request.all_token_ids[0:num_cached_blocks*block_size] to build the BlockStored event's token_ids. This is O(num_cached_blocks) Python-level work plus a potentially large token_ids copy per request per group, added to a hot scheduling path. Since prefix hits are exactly the large-prompt / high-reuse case, the extra per-block extra-keys computation and the full-prefix token slice can add measurable latency to scheduling under high QPS. Note this is only active in the non-default 'full' report mode and behind enable_kv_cache_events, so blast radius is limited; but when enabled it re-does hashing/extra-key work that overlaps with what cache_full_blocks already does.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Scheduler throughput / TTFT under high prefix-cache hit rate with kv_cache_report_mode='full' and enable_kv_cache_events=True vs 'incremental', long shared prompts (e.g. 8k tokens, 90% reuse), many concurrent requests.

findings.claim: In DeepSeekV4Parser._convert_args and DeepSeekV32Parser._convert_args, each streaming arg-converter invocation runs `next((s.name for s in self._tool_slots if s.args == raw_args), None)`. `_convert_args` is called on every streaming tick that flushes/updates argument text (see parser_engine.py:919/962/1017 arg_converter call sites). The `s.args` property joins `_args_parts` into a single string (O(total_args_len)) and then the string equality compares the whole raw_args against every slot's full arg text. As arguments grow long and parallel tool calls accumulate in `_tool_slots`, this becomes O(slots * arg_len) per delta over the stream — repeatedly re-scanning/re-joining full argument buffers to recover a func_name that the engine already knows. This is per-call recompute of a value that should be passed in directly instead of searched by full-string equality.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Stream a single tool call with a large (multi-KB) argument value across many small deltas and measure per-delta time in _convert_args vs number of deltas / arg length.
